title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Given the change of administration and the actions taken by the administration to minimize effective public health measures such as vaccines, I write with trepidation about what will happen in the fall concerning the Sars-CoV-2 vaccines.

We have seen that Covid is not a seasonal virus. The parameters that affect the surges and retreats of this virus are not entirely known, although highly suspected are components of human actions/events, when mutations occur, prevalence of other circulating viruses and bacteria, among other things. We witnessed a substantial Covid infection surge mid-summer into fall in 2024 and then another moderate to substantial surge in the later fall to winter of 2024-2025 along with a very substantial influenza season. Although many health departments stated such a flu year was "not uncommon", when looking to historical data they had to go back at least 15 years to get to the most recent flu season that rivaled it. So, it was an uncommonly infectious year.

Personally, I knew of several people who caught flu early in the winter only to catch it again later in the winter! One person was waiting in the emergency room department waiting area for their daughter to be treated--waiting hours because they couldn't bring her fever down. A few of the other people were part of a childcare situation (one adult, at least two small children) that shut down a child care operation for nearly two weeks because of the outbreak. Additionally, a few of my co-workers were out with flu this year; these are young adults in the prime of their health and they were absent for at least a week to deal with the infection. (Not to mention the constant norovirus outbreaks everywhere this year.) The question of course is: what was different this year that made the flu so much worse and so rampant? Part of the answer may lie in the fact that Sars-CoV-2 infections have also been rampant (that summer surge, the fall/winter surge) and it is documented in the scientific literature that Covid has a dampening effect on the immune system for at least 9 months, such that people become more susceptible to other infectious agents.

So it is even more important to have well matched and timely Sars-CoV-2 vaccines for this summer and fall. Please do everything possible to make sure that the choice of variant, and the manufacturing is not delayed and that the vaccines become available before school starts in the fall.

Also, please do not delay Novavax so that it is not available at the same time as the mRNA vaccines.

And, please make at least two shots available to everyone (approx. 6 months apart) so that everyone has the option of getting vaccinated and having protection all through the year. We know that the vaccine effectiveness drops off after 3-4 months. That means people are not protected for the entire year. So someone getting vaccinated in September is not protected enough in Februrary to avoid the second yearly surge of Covid. We know each infection causes damage, although the damage may be "silent", it is still accumulating damage that may cause health conditions down the road from the Covid infection. If we ever want to truly start to deal with heart disease, dementia disease, etc. we need to take seriously the downstream effects of infections. (For example, we know that measles can cause a condition years later that can kill a seemingly healthy person--Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis, 7-10 years later.)

Additionally, it was very disappointing to see that the influenza components for the vaccine were chosen without an opportunity for public input. Not allowing the public to comment and not allowing the public to watch the meeting about that choice is disgraceful. It is an attempt to disadvantage the public from being aware, knowledgeable, and to have input into their own public health and safety. Shameful. We are of the people and for the people, and the people deserve input and to know what is happening.

title: Comment from Koslap-Petraco, Mary
comment:
-Heartened that we are moving forward with flu vaccine strain selection and manufacturing but concerned about the cancellation of FDA’s VRBPAC meeting and lack of scheduling 2025 meetings.
-Health officials are calling for increased transparency in our process – holding public meetings like VRBPAC and ACIP with independent experts is how we maintain the transparency in our process
-VRBPAC and ACIP meetings give the public a look at latest data on the safety and effectiveness for all of the recommended vaccines as well as new vaccines in development
oWorking groups of expert members first meet to review study data (from all available sources) then present their findings during these public meetings, which are then discussed in detail by members, with the opportunity to ask questions and get clarification before any votes are held.
oDecisions are made after weighing multiple studies, and the quality of the data is always considered and interrogated
oACIP helps make sure our recommendations are based on the preponderance of evidence out there, not cherry-picked studies
-This transparent process and the time and attention committee members spend reviewing high quality data is how we can assuredly promote vaccines.
-We all want to keep our families safe from vaccine preventable diseases AND make sure the recommended vaccines we receive continue to be safe – maintaining the integrity of the process, the standards to which we hold our experts, and the rigor of our scientific studies is critical to doing so.

title: Comment from Caregiver Action Network
comment:
Caregiver Action Network (CAN) appreciates the opportunity to provide public comment on the critical role of immunization in protecting both family caregivers and those they care for. As the nation's leading organization serving the more than 53 million family caregivers across the United States, we strongly urge ACIP and the CDC to continue prioritizing vaccine access and education—particularly regarding COVID-19 and other preventable diseases.

Caregivers serve as the first line of defense for some of the most vulnerable members of our society—older adults, individuals with chronic illnesses, and those with disabilities. Many of these individuals have weakened immune systems or underlying health conditions that make them highly susceptible to severe complications from vaccine-preventable diseases. For them, exposure to infections such as COVID-19, influenza, pneumonia, or shingles can result in hospitalization, long-term health setbacks, or even death.

By ensuring that caregivers receive routine vaccinations, we create a vital layer of protection not only for their own well-being but also for the safety of their loved ones. Immunization is a proven, effective means of reducing transmission risk and preventing unnecessary suffering in caregiving households. The COVID-19 vaccine, in particular, has played an instrumental role in safeguarding high-risk populations and mitigating the devastating impact of the virus on our healthcare system.
We commend ACIP and the CDC for their continued leadership in immunization efforts and urge you to consider the indispensable role that caregivers play in safeguarding public health. Vaccinating caregivers is not just a personal health decision—it is a community-wide responsibility that saves lives.

Thank you for your dedication to protecting public health. We appreciate your ongoing efforts to ensure that all Americans, particularly the most vulnerable among us, have equitable access to life-saving vaccines.

title: Comment from S, M
comment:
I’m writing as a concerned American regarding the halted approval process of the Novavax COVID-19 vaccine by the FDA. This issue is of particular importance to me since I was disabled by Covid, I can only take the Novavax vaccine because I am now immunocompromised and do not react well to mRNA vaccines.

COVID-19 continues to impact millions of Americans even five years on. Cases are still being reported weekly, hospitalizations continue, and Long Covid continues to disable millions of Americans. The need for expanded vaccine options has never been greater.

Novavax offers a non-mRNA alternative that is often more easily tolerated by individuals who have experienced adverse reactions to mRNA vaccines like Pfizer and Moderna. By unexpectedly stopping the approval process, the FDA is limiting access to a vaccine that could provide vital protection to many, especially those who cannot receive mRNA shots. We need this option available now, especially in the face of the current challenges to public health.

The FDA needs to immediately grant Novavax's BLA approval, expand Novavax for pediatric use, and move the expiration date to six or nine months. Millions of Americans are already suffering from Long Covid and we need to have as many tools at our disposal as possible to help prevent millions more from developing this life-altering disability. Delaying Novavax’s approval is not just a public health risk, but a threat to the American economy, which is already facing billions in losses due to the effects of Long Covid.

I appreciate the committee's immediate attention to this important matter.

My comment for the ACIP is to say America is watching. America knows that the current head of HHS has devoted his life to pushing parents to deny their children vaccination. We know Secretary Kennedy helped drop immunization rates in Samoa during their meales epidemic and has taken similar steps during our American measles outbreak. I am writing to urge the FDA and CDC to do the following:
- IMMEDIATELY fully approve the Novavax COVID-19 vaccine through the Biologics License Application (BLA)
- IMMEDIATELY approve Novavax for pediatric use in children 6 months to 12 years

Novavax applied for the BLA a long time ago. Literally millions of doses and dozens of studies as well as multiple clinical trials show that it is safe, effective, and protects for longer and against more variants than mRNA COVID19 vaccines.

This conclusion is well supported by the evidence, and should be considered uncontroversial. Novavax applied a long time ago, and there has been no specific cause to the delay at the FDA. The evidence shows that there is no adequate reason of safety or efficacy for the delay either.

The explanation for this delay is becoming obvious: malfeasance in order to favor other vaccine makers who have a product that is less safe and works less well. The dose in children is the same as the adult dose, so there need be no additional review. With urgency,

title: Comment from Fahrer, Jayme
comment:
Thank you for the opportunity to speak.
My name is Jayme, and I am a survivor of bacterial meningitis. I became ill in 2009, and while I was lucky enough to survive, it forever changed the course of my life. The physical and mental toll this disease has taken — from the pain and trauma of hospitalization to the ongoing emotional and neurological after-effects — is something I live with every day.
I am also an art teacher and someone who works closely with children, families, and the broader community. My work is rooted in healing, wellness, and expression — and I see firsthand the importance of prevention when it comes to devastating, preventable illnesses like meningitis.
I urge the committee to continue prioritizing comprehensive vaccine recommendations, including the MenACWY and MenB vaccines, especially for adolescents and young adults. These are critical windows for prevention, and awareness alone is not enough. Too many families still don’t know there are two separate vaccines needed to be fully protected.
The current recommendation structure has led to significant consequences.
A troubling increase in meningococcal cases: In 2023, the United States experienced a 10-year-high of 438 confirmed and probable cases. Source
In 2025, three states – Alabama, Ohio, and Virginia – experienced cases of meningococcal disease, and as recently as a few weeks ago, a college student at the University of Virginia passed away from Meningitis B.
An unacceptably low MenB vaccine uptake rate—just 13% of eligible adolescents have completed the MenB vaccine series, compared to over 90% for MenACWY. Source
Since 2011, 100% of all college meningococcal outbreaks in the U.S. have been caused by MenB.
Only 6 out of 10 17-year-olds have received at least two doses of the MenACWY vaccine.
Now is the time to strengthen meningococcal prevention in the U.S. – 45% of healthcare providers surveyed believe there is too much complexity surrounding meningococcal vaccination compared to other vaccine-preventable childhood diseases. Source
We applaud ACIP’s review of GSK’s pentavalent vaccine, which has the potential to simplify the vaccination process and improve uptake. Additionally, the potential label expansion for MenQuadfi in infants could protect our youngest and most vulnerable populations.
We urge you to continue this momentum by reprioritizing the review of current meningitis vaccination recommendations so that all Americans are protected from this unpredictable and deadly disease.
I am here not only as a survivor, but as someone who believes no one should have to go through what I did — or worse, lose their life — from a disease we can prevent.
Thank you for your time, and for the life-saving work you do.

title: Comment from Pennsylvania Immunization Coalition
comment:
2025 April ACIP Meeting
On behalf of the Pennsylvania Immunization Coalition, we are happy to hear that the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices has resumed its meetings and will continue to provide timely updates regarding vaccine recommendations. We would like to express our gratitude to the many experts and public health professionals in this committee. Please know that the work that you all do matters and helps keep our communities safe.
In the era of vaccine hesitancy, where fears about the COVID vaccine have now percolated to other routine vaccinations such as influenza and measles vaccines, we hope that the ACIP will continue to advocate for evidence-based recommendations. Vaccines remain the triumph of modern medicine. Childhood diseases such as Haemophilus influenzae type B and Rotavirus, that caused so much suffering and death in the past, have all but disappeared due to effective vaccination.
The Pennsylvania Immunization Coalition is concerned that we as a nation are now moving backwards with regard to infectious diseases and vaccinations. Take the recent rise in Measles cases, for example. As of March 27, 2025, there were a total of 483 confirmed measles cases reported by 20 jurisdictions and 97% of those people were unvaccinated (or had unknown vaccination status).
As one of the country’s founding fathers, Benjamin Franklin, said, “An ounce of prevention is worth a pound of cure”. Let’s make America Healthy Again by starting with prevention of disease by vaccines. We need national leadership to reinforce the great effectiveness of vaccines, as well as address any parental/caregiver concerns regarding vaccine safety in a compassionate and evidence-based manner. We look forward to working with this committee and other national and state leaders to develop and deliver this message and protect the future health of our great nation. Thank you

title: Comment from Roppolo, Rebecca
comment:
Rebecca Roppolo, MPH. Mother and Scientist.

Maintaining Integrity of the Vaccine Approval and Recommendation Process
As a mother of two small children, I rely on the vaccine recommendation process to make informed, confident decisions about how to protect my family. And as someone who has worked in a vaccine development laboratory at the NIH and holds a Master of Public Health, I deeply understand the importance of both scientific rigor and transparency.
•I’m heartened to see flu vaccine strain selection moving forward, but I’m concerned by the cancellation of VRBPAC meetings and the lack of scheduling for 2025. Public trust in vaccines hinges on seeing science in action—not behind closed doors.
•Public, expert-driven meetings like VRBPAC and ACIP are not just bureaucratic steps; they are essential mechanisms for public trust.
•These meetings show the process: working groups carefully review all available evidence, ask critical questions, and engage in open dialogue before any votes occur.
•The process isn’t about cherry-picking data—it’s about weighing the totality of evidence to ensure recommendations are based on the best science available.
Support for Current COI Policies
The vaccine review process should be above reproach—but it should also value expertise.
•I support ACIP’s current conflict of interest policies, which strike the right balance between integrity and insight.
•Disclosure requirements, financial divestment, recusal from voting—these guardrails are in place and working.
•Scientists who have dedicated their careers to vaccine research should not be disqualified from contributing just because they have expertise. Transparency allows us to benefit from their knowledge while managing potential bias.
The Cost of Not Vaccinating
As a parent, I understand the desire to protect our children. But the heartbreaking measles-related death of a child in the U.S.—the first in decades—is a tragic reminder of what happens when fear outweighs facts.
•The risks of vaccinating are extremely small. The risks of not vaccinating are growing, and very real.
•When people hesitate or refuse vaccines, they often underestimate the diseases we're trying to prevent. We’ve forgotten what a world without vaccines looked like—when children routinely died from diseases like measles, meningitis, and polio.
•These ACIP meetings are a chance to weigh those risks openly, honestly, and with the best available data. We need to keep holding them—and we need the public to hear them.
Meningococcal Vaccine Recommendations
As someone who has worked in vaccine science and now guides my own children’s care, I know how confusing vaccine schedules can be—and how dangerous that confusion can become.
•Meningitis B is deadly and on the rise. Yet only 13% of eligible adolescents have completed the MenB series.
•It’s time to simplify and strengthen our recommendations. We know complexity hinders uptake—and young people are paying the price.
•I applaud the committee’s work to review combination vaccines and call on ACIP to continue prioritizing meningococcal vaccine evaluation.
I am here as a scientist, a public health professional, and most importantly, a mother. I want to thank ACIP members—past and present—for your commitment to rigorous, transparent, evidence-based work. Please keep meeting. Please keep making this process public. Our families depend on it.

title: Comment from Head and Neck Cancer Alliance
comment:
On behalf of the Head and Neck Cancer Alliance, a nonprofit committed to advancing prevention, detection, treatment, and rehabilitation of head and neck cancers, we want to highlight the critical importance of HPV vaccination as a proven tool for preventing head and neck cancers, especially oropharyngeal cancers, which are rising in the U.S.—particularly among men. Each year, HPV causes approximately 36,000 cases of cancer in the U.S., and over 10,000 of those are head and neck cancers. We have a vaccine that’s more than 90% effective at preventing the HPV strains that cause these cancers. We support efforts to optimize the HPV vaccine schedule, including the potential expansion of the recommended routine vaccination window to ages 9 to 12. This shift would:
•Provide a clear, consistent benchmark for clinicians and families,
•Allow more time to complete the series by age 13,
•Capitalize on a stronger immune response at younger ages.
Any changes must be grounded in robust data. We urge ACIP to ensure any future dosing or schedule recommendations are supported by high-quality evidence that accounts for these risks. In closing, we thank ACIP for your careful, transparent, and evidence-driven approach. Your work is essential to public trust and public health—and it directly impacts the future we envision: one where fewer families face the devastating consequences of HPV-related head and neck cancers. Thank you.

I am also asking for a Novavax expansion for pediatric use and for the expiration date moved from 3 to either 6 or 9 months. Children spread the virus to entire households. It is important that they be protected. There is much vaccine hesitancy around mRNA, but given an option, especially with full approval, we might see more uptake with pediatric Novavax than we have with the mRNA alternatives. The pediatric vaccine is the same as the adult vaccine that already has EUA.

I also am requesting that the updated vaccine be available for the end of the summer, before the kids go back to school. Unfortunately, children shed a lot of virus and spend their days in crowded, usually insufficiently ventilated spaces where viruses can easily spread.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Dear Members of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP),

I am writing to express my strong support for childhood immunizations and the continued work of the ACIP in ensuring that vaccines remain a cornerstone of public health for our country.. Immunizations have proven to be one of the most effective ways to protect children from preventable diseases, safeguarding their health and well-being, as well as contributing to the overall safety of our communities.

I have been a practicing pediatrician for 22 years and I feel strongly that immunizations are the best thing I do to keep my patients healthy. Training in medical school and residency in the 1990s and early 2000s I never thought I would see measles resurgence in our country. And yet, here we are -confronting outbreaks of this deadly and very preventable disease.

Of course parents want to keep their children safe, this includes parents who choose not to vaccinate. Often, the choice not to vaccinate children is based on incorrect assessments that vaccines themselves are more risky than the diseases they prevent. These incorrect assessments continue as myths about vaccines being harmful are perpetuated and the true risks of dangerous diseases (such as measles) are downplayed. The ACIP serves a vital role of continuously reviewing data to weigh the risks of not vaccinating with the risks of vaccinating and communicating this important information.

I encourage the ACIP to continue to support and advocate for the availability, accessibility, and acceptance of vaccines, ensuring that all children, regardless of their background, have the opportunity to grow up healthy and protected. I also appreciate the committee’s ongoing efforts to communicate the importance of vaccines to the public and support policies that promote vaccination as an essential part of childhood healthcare.

Sincerely,
Jennifer Betz, MD
Denver, CO

title: Comment from Wagner, Karen
comment: Vaccines are imperative for personal and for public health. Please keep research current, vaccines current, and have the vaccines widely distributed throughout the United States. I always want the choice of whether to be vaccinated. That is a personal and not a governmental decision.

title: Comment from Fields, Samantha
comment:
As someone who depends on our public health infrastructure to stay safe, and as a passionate advocate for equity in healthcare, I want to express my strong support for the continued integrity, transparency, and scientific rigor of the ACIP and the vaccine recommendation process.

I’m deeply heartened that we are continuing forward with flu vaccine strain selection and manufacturing. However, I am also concerned by the cancellation of the FDA's VRBPAC meeting and the lack of scheduling for upcoming meetings in 2025. The public deserves full transparency, and meetings like VRBPAC and ACIP provide essential opportunities to see and understand how decisions are being made about the vaccines that keep our communities safe. These meetings allow for detailed discussions of safety and effectiveness data, with time for questions, clarification, and real scientific discourse—not headlines or soundbites.

As a Medicaid member and a person living with multiple chronic illnesses, I understand how critical it is to feel confident in the medical decisions made on our behalf. That confidence comes from knowing those decisions were made with care, data, and independence. ACIP’s process is robust—expert working groups comb through mountains of research before anything is recommended, and they don’t just rely on one study or one source. They consider the full body of evidence, and that’s what makes the process trustworthy.

I also want to voice my appreciation for the strong conflict of interest (COI) policies already in place. Public trust hinges on knowing that ACIP members are not influenced by industry, and the current disclosure and recusal policies help ensure this. Transparency doesn’t mean pretending no one has ever worked on a vaccine—it means owning that experience and showing the public exactly how decisions are made and who is making them. I find it reassuring, not troubling, that we have experts who’ve dedicated their careers to understanding these complex issues guiding the discussion.

I’m especially grateful to see the recent measles outbreak added to this meeting’s agenda. As heartbreaking as it is to say, a child has died from measles in our country—something that hasn’t happened in over twenty years. This should be a wake-up call. The fear that leads some parents to skip vaccinations is real, but it’s based on misinformation and mistrust, not science. ACIP’s work helps counter that by laying out clear, evidence-based guidance that allows families to make truly informed decisions.

When vaccines work, nothing happens. No illness, no hospitalizations, no funerals. But that “nothing” only happens because of the “something” you all do—evaluating, recommending, communicating, and protecting public health.

Please continue to convene regularly and publicly. Please continue upholding the integrity of this process and prioritizing the health of our children, our elders, our communities, and those of us who rely most on public systems to survive.

Thank you to the current and past ACIP members for your time, your expertise, and your commitment to public health.

title: Comment from J, S
comment:
Dear Members of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP),

I am writing to submit my public comment regarding the immunizations currently under review by the ACIP for Docket No. CDC-2025-0017. As a concerned citizen, I am deeply worried about the current administration’s approach to vaccines, particularly regarding vaccine research and development, promotion, and accessibility. I would like to offer my feedback for your consideration.

The need for robust vaccination policies is urgent, as there are numerous concerns that demand attention. The following list highlights just a few of the pressing issues among a much broader range of challenges, many of which stem from the dismantling of previously effective policies:

* Child flu deaths are at an all-time high, as reported by the CDC: https://www.cdc.gov/flu/whats-new/2023-2024-pediatric-deaths-200.html

* Measles cases are surging in the U.S., despite the disease being declared eliminated in 2000, with 2 deaths reported in 2025, putting many more children at risk. https://www.cdc.gov/measles/data-research/index.html

* COVID-19 deaths in the U.S. have surpassed 1.2 million, a staggering loss: https://covid.cdc.gov/covid-data-tracker/#trends_totaldeaths_select_00

* Vaccines have been proven to protect our communities from these and other preventable diseases: https://www.hhs.gov/immunization/basics/work/protection/index.html

In light of these and many related concerns, I respectfully request the following actions:

1. Approve updated COVID vaccines to match circulating strains for all available vaccines, including mRNA vaccines.

2. Recommend that the FDA approve Novavax's COVID-19 vaccine for use under Biologics License Application (BLA) for all ages 6 months and older.

3. Approve COVID vaccine eligibility for all ages 6 months and older for the 2025-2026 formulation, with availability beginning early (ideally in July-August 2025, prior to the start of K-12 schools).

4. Approve twice-yearly COVID vaccines for all ages 6 months and older (fall and spring).

5. Approve influenza vaccine eligibility for all ages 6 months and older for Fall 2025.

6. Recommend that the CDC publicly and routinely emphasize the importance of vaccinating individuals against highly contagious and potentially deadly diseases, including but not limited to COVID, measles, and influenza.

7. Recommend that the CDC publish forecasts of data and evidence on the spread of contagious and potentially deadly diseases.

I strongly advocate for the federal government to uphold the integrity of the entire vaccine process, from research and development to approval, recommendation, production, and distribution. It is crucial that this process remain transparent to the public, ensuring clear communication and accountability at every step. Furthermore, I believe it is essential to preserve opportunities for public comment and engagement, facilitating ongoing dialogue and trust between the government and the people it serves.

Thank you for considering my comment. I trust that my input, along with those of other concerned citizens and professionals, will be taken into account while making decisions that will benefit the health and well-being of our nation.

title: Comment from Mendelsohn, Jason
comment: My name is Jason Mendelsohn, and I am survivor of HPV related tonsil cancer. I was diagnosed at the age of 44, with no other symptoms but a small bump that appeared on my neck out of nowhere. I was told at that time, that I likely came in contact with the virus in college around the age of 19, however the virus likely laid dormant in the crypts of my throat for decades, eventually surfacing as cancer.  I have been married to my wife Ronni for 28 years, and we have three awesome kids; boy/girls twins that are 23 and a son who is 17. When I was diagnosed with oropharyngeal cancer in 2014 our twins were 12 and our son was 6. I want you to understand my love of family, because shortly after beginning radiation and chemo treatments, just after having a radical tonsillectomy and neck dissection, 42 lymph nodes removed from my neck. I made videos to our three kids saying goodbye. Those videos went something like this, “One day you’re going to get married, I’m not going to be there, and this is what’s most important.” I made those videos, over and over again, thinking that if they were the last things my kids ever heard from me, I wanted them to be perfect.  On a side note, while going through radiation treatment five days a week for seven weeks, with third degree burns in my throat and ulcers in my mouth, combined with chemotherapy one day a week for seven weeks, my friends called me Superman, stating that I was tough like him. I mention this, as I adopted the name SupermanHPV and launched my SupermanHPV.com website, to provide inspiration and information for those diagnosed and/or researching, HPV, the HPV vaccine and HPV related cancers. I continue to speak with approximately 2-4 patients, survivors or caregivers weekly from all over the world to provide them with the peace of mind that they to can get through the treatment and that they will be ok. I always remind them that what they are going through is temporary. People always tell me how scared they are, and that I give them hope. I share all of this with you to state that unless there is documented proof and good science behind reducing the number of doses a young boy or girl should receive to protect them from HPV preventable cancers, all of them, not just cervical cancer, I would urge you not to change the current dose schedule. As a father, I believe my role and the role of other parents is to protect our children. The HPV vaccine at the current dose schedule, two shots before 15 and three shots thereafter, has the science behind it, that protects our children. Why even consider changing the recommended dose schedule, if the science isn’t there for cancers such as oropharyngeal, anal, penile, vulvar and/or vaginal?  It simply makes me uncomfortable and nervous that a vaccine that is proven to protect our children from HPV preventable cancers would have its doses reduced, without evidence that that reduced recommendation would still protect our children at the same success rate. To take this one step further, I also have concerns about reducing the number of doses, as I speak with people on a monthly basis that are also dealing with reoccurrence, where their oropharyngeal cancer has now spread to their lungs and brain. While the HPV vaccine was not around when I was a young boy, it is now and the science is proven. I want you to understand that I am overjoyed and grateful that the HPV vaccine exists, and that my three kids are protected from HPV preventable cancers, a virus that three out of four adults by thirty have been exposed to. I want all children to be protected worldwide from HPV preventable cancers, as I believe it’s our responsibility to protect them. Please leave the current dose schedule in place, until you are confident that a reduced schedule will protect our children from all of the cancers caused by HPV. Thank you.

title: Comment from National Association of Pediatric Nurse Practitioners
organization: National Association of Pediatric Nurse Practitioners
comment:
The National Association of Pediatric Nurse Practitioners (NAPNAP) calls on all federal and state agencies, including the CDC, to rely on widely accepted, evidence-based guidelines and well-designed scientific research to set health care policies. Ensuring transparency and scientific integrity in policy development requires convening public meetings for advisory groups such as ACIP, whose independent scholars and experts carefully analyze diverse data sources before making informed recommendations. Inviting public comment strengthens the policymaking process. To enhance these critical discussions, NAPNAP strongly recommends including advanced practice nurses who specialize in pediatric care as voting participants.

Vaccination is the most effective and cost-efficient strategy for preventing illness and saving lives. It is essential that decision-makers within the DHHS and its agencies recognize 200 years of evidence demonstrating that vaccines have saved millions of lives and significantly reduced health care costs by preventing severe illness and infection. The U.S. must collaborate with the global health care community to combat the spread of infectious diseases and administer vaccines to save lives. Additionally, to empower health care providers and educate patient families, the CDC should reinstate the timely publication of evidence-based guidance ensuring we work together to prevent the spread of illness and improve public health outcomes.

NAPNAP supports all evidence-based efforts to have every infant, child, adolescent, and adult receive all age-appropriate immunizations on time. We are pleased that ACIP’s agenda includes discussions related to critical vaccines, including those to protect against influenza, COVID-19 and RSV, to mitigate respiratory illnesses and deaths in infants, children, adolescents and their families.

NAPNAP commends ACIP’s commitment to cancer prevention through HPV immunization. Any modifications to routine vaccine recommendations – whether regarding initiation or dosage – should be guided by a rigorous, scholarly review of the current, high-quality research, with a focus on increasing vaccine uptake and reducing the 360,000 HPV-related cancer cases diagnosed annually.

Although rare, our members have seen first-hand how meningitis can be a potentially serious disease that can quickly cause brain or organ damage, loss of limbs or death, as in the recent case of a UVA student. While there are vaccines for the five strains of meningococcal disease, data as of 2021 suggests that up to 40% of teens are not fully immunized against A, C, W and Y although 2-dose vaccines are recommended at age 11-12 and age 16. Vaccine rates for meningitis B are much lower with only about 3 out of 10 teens being immunized.

Our member experts feel it is critical that ACIP simplify and streamline meningitis vaccine recommendations based on safety and efficacy data. NAPNAP urges ACIP leaders to prioritize the review of current meningitis vaccination recommendations and clear, concise communications so that clinicians can administer vaccines to protect their patients from this unpredictable and deadly disease. The routine vs. shared clinical decision-making recommendations for meningococcal vaccines can be confusing. According to a study published in Vaccine, the majority of surveyed parents were unaware of available vaccines for MenACWY or MenB. Even some clinicians are unsure about ACIP’s recommendations for meningococcal vaccines.

Our members report frustration about the shared decision-making model, saying it is hard to counsel patients to receive a vaccine that is not prioritized at the same level as other child and adolescent vaccines. Based on clinical experience, our members support dosing recommendations that are clear and consistent to encourage uptake while limiting vaccine type and dosage confusion for providers and patient families.

As ACIP considers meningitis recommendations, we strongly encourage the committee to recognize that families with younger adolescents are more likely to adhere to annual well visits. Therefore, we think it is critical to include meningitis vaccine recommendations starting at age 10 for those considering the pentavalent option and continuing to recommend to all 11–12 year-olds who did not initiate vaccination previously. We look to the committee to provide strong guidance on how to effectively utilize the available meningitis vaccines in a cost-effective manner so that practices can stock and recommend the vaccines for all eligible patients. NAPNAP urges the committee to consider how all meningitis vaccines can and will be accessible and administered in an equitable way to best protect all adolescents regardless of their families’ ability to pay.

We urge the CDC and ACIP to remain strong partners in delivering clear, evidence-based guidance on vaccine safety and efficacy, ensuring the public understands the life-saving benefits of vaccines.

title: Comment from Meyer, Laura
comment:
I am writing as the mother to a seven week old baby. My family is deeply concerned with the current measles outbreak and with increasing vaccine hesitancy. Vaccines are not just an individual choice - it is something we all need to do to create herd immunity, to protect those who cannot be vaccinated, like my baby and those who are immuno-compromised. Encouraging vaccination will help my family feel safe, and protect my baby from serious illness or even death. Thank you.

Please don't let another person die because they didn't know they could prevent Meningitis.

title: Comment from Sundling, Kaitlin
comment:
I have no conflicts of interest to disclose. As a physician-scientist (MD-PhD) and public health advocate, I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

As a pathologist who specializes in cervical cancer screening, I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Hello ACIP CDC Members,

I’m a senior who can look back to the days when there was public consensus that vaccines could prevent not only the worst childhood diseases, but also their sequelae. How many seniors like me understand that we still remain at high risk of severe outcomes - including death and disability - from acute COVID-19 and Long Covid, and need an updated COVID-19 vaccine every 6 months? How many pharmacists understand that just because we may have been born before 1957 we are only presumptively immune to measles, mumps and rubella? That those of us who have no record of being infected or vaccinated are at high risk of severe outcomes?

Please support continued vaccine development, approval, and recommendations for all age groups.

Thank you,

Marcia Cohen Zakai

title: Comment from Marso, Andy
comment: My name is Andy Marso, I'm a journalist, author, and survivor of meningococcal disease. I contracted the disease in 2004 when I was a senior at the University of Kansas. Within 24 hours I went from being perfectly healthy and feeling fine to being airlifted to a Level 1 trauma center, in multiple organ failure with disseminated intravascular coagulation, not expected to survive. I spent three weeks in the ICU on a ventilator. When I was brought out of sedation I was confronted with the unimaginable: most of my limbs were blackened and dead. I had tissue damage equal to third-degree burns over 30% of my body. Over the next three months I endured excruciating debridement procedures and skin grafting to save my arms and legs. Despite the treatments, doctors were forced to amputate all of my toes and all of my fingers except my right thumb. I then spent a year in physical and occupational therapy, learning to walk and take care of myself again. My medical bills that first year ran to almost $2 million. I'm writing to encourage ACIP to adopt a meningitis vaccine recommendation that will prevent as many people as possible from going through what I went through. My hope is that it will be a recommendation that is clear, concise, and easily communicated to the general public. The current bifurcated recommendation is needlessly complicated and has resulted in extremely low uptake of the meningococcal B vaccine. This is counterproductive to public health, given that meningococcal B (the serogroup that caused my infection) can cause all of the same terrible sequelae as the other serogroups. We can do better. I'm enclosing a few photos from my time in the hospital.

title: Comment from Alliance for Women's Health and Prevention
organization: Alliance for Women's Health and Prevention
comment:
The Alliance for Women’s Health and Prevention (AWHP) is a non-profit advocacy organization focused on advancing policies that support preventive health for all women and girls. Given the critical role of vaccines in preventing disease and allowing women to live their healthiest lives, AWHP supports efforts that advance vaccine access and uptake.

AWHP is a strong believer in the value of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) and the key role that it plays in upholding transparency and scientific rigor, providing the public with the latest high-quality data on safety and effectiveness for both recommended vaccines and new vaccines in development. The ACIP plays a critical role in ascertaining that vaccine recommendations are based on in-depth evidence that is thoroughly reviewed, and public meetings give individuals the opportunity to provide comment and review the data first-hand and better understand that vaccines are only recommended when the benefits outweigh the risks. We all want to keep our families and loved ones safe from vaccine-preventable diseases — and maintaining the integrity of the recommendations process, the standards to which we hold our experts, and the rigor of our scientific studies is critical to doing so.

AWHP is concerned about the continued outbreak of measles, and thanks the ACIP for adding the discussion of the outbreak to its agenda. As the nation reels from three deaths due to measles this year — equaling the number recorded in the previous 24 years combined — we must prioritize keeping our families safe through vaccination. While vaccines, like all medical interventions, come with the risk of rare side effects, the benefits far outweigh these small risks. Simply put, when vaccines work, nothing happens — yet people are protected, and they remain safe from deadly diseases. As such, vaccines are a critical part of our preventive health efforts, and we must uphold efforts to keep them accessible and promote their uptake.

This outbreak and the increases in cases of other infectious diseases underscore the unpredictability of vaccine-preventable diseases and the growing need for clear, consistent, and practical immunization recommendations that are feasible to implement in clinical practice. When guidelines are complex, healthcare providers struggle to implement these recommendations, and the public is left vulnerable to not only disease, but also misinformation.

To that end, AWHP also strongly urges the ACIP to reprioritize their assessment of meningococcal vaccine recommendations to ensure that more adolescents and young adults are protected from this deadly disease. The current meningococcal vaccination schedule is complicated, with suboptimal vaccination rates reported across MenACWY and particularly MenB vaccines. With new pentavalent meningococcal vaccination options, now is the time to strengthen our prevention toolbox against all five types of meningococcal bacteria most likely to cause meningococcal meningitis, and to continue input on the overall platform review of the adolescent meningococcal vaccine schedule.

The ACIP plays a crucial role in protecting America’s public health, and we commend your continued dedication to evaluating and setting immunization recommendations that safeguard our communities, prevent disease outbreaks, and ensure access to life-saving vaccines. We thank current and past ACIP members for their time and expertise, and urge the Committee to continue to hold public meetings at a regular cadence.

To the members of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP):

I am a concerned citizen who takes the health of herself and her family very seriously. My family, including my young children, are all up-to-date with every vaccine for which we are eligible, including the annual updated vaccines for COVID-19 and influenza.

We are grateful for these mRNA vaccines and their protection, but they made us both feel absolutely terrible with different reactions, enough that it took a few days for each of us to recover, disrupting our lives and having to cancel plans including our young children. Since its approval in 2023, we have only sought out and received Novavax vaccines, which is protein-based instead of mRNA-based. We have not had any reactions to the Novavax vaccine, thankfully. I would like BLA approval for Novavax. My children have not had any reactions to any of their mRNA-based COVID-19 vaccines, thankfully. Novavax has demonstrated a strong safety profile, high efficacy, and lower reactogenicity than the mRNA-based COVID-19 vaccines. The FDA has the data provided to them and at least 2 years of data from Novavax in commercial use.

I am asking the committee to consider the following:

Novavax COVID-19 vaccine Biologics License Application (BLA) approval.
Expansion for pediatric use of the Novavax COVID-19 vaccine.
Expiration date of the Novavax COVID-19 vaccine moved from three to six or nine months.

title: Comment from Shelstein, Dana
comment:
It is long past time for the FDA to grant full approval to Novavax, including for pediatric use and with an extended expiration window. Novavax has consistently demonstrated an excellent safety profile, both in clinical trials and real-world use, with significantly fewer adverse reactions than mRNA vaccines—especially when it comes to myocarditis. Its traditional protein-based platform provides an urgently needed non-mRNA alternative for those with medical, allergic, or personal concerns, and is more acceptable globally to vaccine-hesitant populations.

Crucially, Novavax offers broad-spectrum and long-lasting protection against COVID-19, including newer variants, making it an invaluable tool in our public health arsenal as the virus continues to evolve. It also maintains strong cellular immunity, which is especially important for long-term defense and cross-variant protection.

The FDA’s continued delay in fully approving Novavax—despite overwhelming evidence of its safety and efficacy—is unacceptable and undermines public trust. Millions of Americans want and need this option, especially those who remain unvaccinated due to hesitation around mRNA, and those seeking more durable protection. Pediatric approval is especially critical, as parents deserve the right to choose a safe, effective, and traditional vaccine for their children.

The FDA must stop dragging its feet. Give Novavax the full approval it has earned, expand access to children, and extend expiration dates to ensure doses aren’t wasted due to unnecessary bureaucratic delays. This is a matter of scientific integrity, public health, and medical freedom.

Even if a vaccine targets a single antigen, this antigen targets multiple parts of the virus and as we see with every updated Covid vaccine it targets multiple variants not just one variant, providing essential protection from COVID-19 infection and severe disease and long COVID. Regardless of vaccine type, the ultimate goal is to protect ourselves and others from the virus. The scientific community's commitment to vaccines has been pivotal in our fight against the pandemic, and I encourage the FDA to recognize the value of having multiple vaccine options available to the public. I would like to see Novavax fully approved, I would like Novavax to be available for pediatrics, for extended expiration dates. title: Comment from Association of Immunization Managers
comment:
The Association of Immunization Managers, a 501(c)3 non-profit member organization that supports the managers of the 64 federally funded immunization programs across the 50 states, 8 US territories and freely-associated states, and 6 major US cities, would like to extend its thanks to the members of the ACIP and its work groups for the important work you undertake to make recommendations that are based upon the latest and most trustworthy data available at the time. Since its establishment in 1964, the expert members of the ACIP have reviewed, discussed, and debated the research, data, costs, and benefits of recommending vaccines for public use. Their process is transparent, potential conflicts of interest are appropriately disclosed and reconciled, and their recommendations are informed by their collective expertise in vaccine science and infectious diseases. Committee members, as well as work group members and liaisons, volunteer their time to do this critical work because they believe in the mission of protecting the public from infectious diseases that can be prevented through vaccines and vaccine-like products.
Among the topics of discussion during this meeting is a review of the data around reducing the number of recommended doses of Human Papillomavirus (HPV) vaccine. This is the second time the ACIP has reviewed research and data to consider eliminating a dose of this highly effective vaccine. In 2016, the ACIP voted to reduce the number of HPV vaccines administered to children ages 9-14 from three doses to two doses. Nine years later, the Committee will discuss the evidence supporting further reducing the recommendation to just one dose for some populations and reducing the recommendation from three doses to two doses for other populations. The Committee’s willingness to review these data and consider recommendations to eliminate vaccine doses is an important example of the members’ dedication to providing evidence-based recommendations that inform the Centers for Disease Control’s (CDC’s) recommended vaccine schedules. Similarly, past recommendations of the ACIP resulted in the elimination of an influenza B strain from seasonal influenza vaccines (2024), a change in polio vaccine recommendations from the oral, live virus vaccine to the safer, injected, inactivated polio vaccine (2000), and removal of the Rotashield vaccine against rotavirus due to a safety issue that was identified through the CDC’s Vaccine Adverse Event Reporting System (VAERS) (1999). The work of the ACIP not only provides important recommendations for the prevention of infectious diseases but also protects the public from unnecessary or potentially harmful vaccine products.
AIM looks forward to the continued work of the ACIP, the transparency of its decision-making process, and the expertise it provides to professionals across public health and healthcare. The work of these highly experienced and knowledgeable Committee members is critical to the health and wellbeing of all Americans.

title: Comment from Vaughan, Patrick
comment:
Hi, my name is Patrick Vaughan, I am an engineer and scientist, and I possess no conflicts of interest.
What we need to keep:
1.Everyone needs to be able to access these vaccines, they need to be universally available for 6 months and up.
2.Immunocompromised individuals need to continue to be allowed a covid vaccine every 2 months.
3.We need to keep the 65+ additional dose vaccine recommendations.

I have 6 points I feel we need to add in addition to these requirements.
What we need to add to this:
1.We need to update the covid vaccine frequency to every 3-4 months.

On average the active immunity nearly completely wanes within 3-4 months after receiving it, leaving you basically unprotected after the time that the active immunity has worn off. You’re just left with memory immunity at that point. The natural solution to this is to get a covid vaccine every 3-4 months. We know there is no harm in doing this due to the German man that was vaccinated 217 times.

2.We need to update the covid vaccine formula every 6 months as opposed to once every year.
Due to the unchecked spread of covid, we are allowing it to mutate and evolve exponentially faster, meaning that prior immunity is waning at ever faster rates as the virus moves sufficiently far away to reinfect individuals. As covid is not seasonal, having a summer and winter wave at minimum, we need to utilize the speed of mRNA technology to update the vaccines at a faster rate to keep covid in check. This may even be feasible with Novavax protein-based technology, while it will definitely be more difficult for them, it is possible within a 6-month timeframe.
3.On a every 6-month updated vaccine schedule, the covid vaccine frequency should be set to every 3 months.

This will allow individuals the ability to receive an updated vaccine and a booster for each vaccine update at the 6-month intervals.

4.Allow all immunocompromised individuals to receive multiple vaccine boosters a year, as opposed to limiting it to moderately or severely immunocompromised individuals.
Any immune compromising condition should be allowed to get a vaccine every 2 months with doctor approval, as even if you’re “mildly” immunocompromised, you’re still immunocompromised.
5.Individuals with low BMI (underweight, under 18.5) and High BMI (overweight and obese, greater than or equal to 30), even if immune competent, should be allowed multiple vaccine doses a year.
We know that if you have low or high BMI, that your vaccine effectiveness is going to wane much faster than if you have normal BMI. These are known people that have shortened vaccine efficacy windows, meaning they have a shortened duration of protection.

6.Additional booster doses for pregnant individuals within the third trimester of pregnancy.

As newly born infants can’t get vaccinated until 6 months, maternal covid vaccination is their only protection against severe disease through the passage of covid vaccine antibodies in utero.

title: Comment from Hinton, Jennifer

Novavax provides a critically important option for individuals who, for medical, personal, or philosophical reasons, seek a protein-based vaccine platform rather than mRNA technology. Novavax’s vaccine has demonstrated strong efficacy and safety, and it plays a valuable role for those who were hesitant to receive mRNA vaccines or who experienced adverse reactions. Removing or limiting access to this option would leave many without a viable alternative, potentially decreasing overall vaccination rates.

Ensuring equitable access to multiple vaccine technologies respects patient autonomy, strengthens public confidence, and supports a more resilient healthcare system.

title: Comment from Puhak, Blake
comment:
To the CDC ACIP committee:

I have no conflicts of interest to disclose. I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

I am writing as a doctoral prepared public and population health nurse, having been in nursing for over 40 years. Unfortunately, public health is being unraveled with mass firings of federal public health workers, and funding cuts to research and programs, including research focused on vaccine hesitancy. It is critically important that all members of this committee speak out at every opportunity to support safe and effective vaccines. It is also crucial for everyone to write their elected officials to let them know how important it is that people have access to vaccines for public health and disease prevention. If we do not have prevention and public health with immunizations, there will only be more disability and deaths, and more expensive health care, along with the loss of people who will not be able to contribute to the workforce. Please continue the robust epidemiological science discussions around vaccinations, vaccination studies, and as a committee continue to assess the data to make the recommendations and encourage the robust availability of vaccines, and funding needed to keep our populations healthy and strong. title: Comment from Buher, Lori
comment:
Dear Members of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP):
As a mother of a meningococcal survivor and former Secretary of the National Meningitis Association (NMA), I am writing to ask the committee to consider strengthening the current meningococcal immunization recommendations to include routine immunization for all strains, including B. I have shared my son Carl’s story many times in this forum, and I continue to do so because it speaks to the long-term health impairments many meningococcal survivors face. My hope is that by sharing my son’s story again, the committee will understand the true risks of this disease and why preventing it is so important.

It is difficult to express to those who have not witnessed meningococcal disease firsthand, just how much it impacts the victim and their family. My son, Carl Buher, was a young, healthy football player enjoying his first year of high school. He came home from football practice one day, complaining he didn’t feel well. The next day, he developed flu-like symptoms, and by the afternoon, he had developed purple splotches all over his face and arms. We rushed him to the local hospital. By pure luck, the doctor on call had attended a recent medical conference and was able to quickly diagnose Carl with meningococcemia.

From there, he was immediately airlifted to the Seattle Children’s Hospital. His heart stopped twice on the way and a third time at the hospital. He was put into a drug-induced coma that lasted for three weeks. As a result of the infection, Carl spent five months in the hospital, lost three fingers and both his legs below the knee. He’s had 11 surgeries, and it took him four years before he could walk again using prosthetics – half of his teenage years. In the blink of an eye, my healthy and happy 14-year-old son had his world turned upside down. No longer was he able to play football or easily do many of the things he so enjoyed.

Those of us whose children survive meningococcal disease consider ourselves fortunate, but life is not easy for our children, who often face lifelong challenges and health consequences. Last year, Carl underwent a transplant operation, receiving a kidney from a college friend, after several years of medications and more than a year of nightly peritoneal dialysis. Today, breakdown of the skin grafts on his stumps continue to cause challenges, requiring many visits to his Prosthetist. Imagine facing each day with the challenges our survivors face.
While Carl is blessed with many gifts – a fruitful career, a supportive wife and two healthy young children – he still feels the impact of this disease every single day. This impact extends beyond the physical challenges many survivors face; there are also economic implications. The cost of living is often higher for survivors because of the ongoing medical support and accommodations needed.

I implore this committee to keep this top of mind as prevention strategies are considered. We have seen the positive impact routine meningococcal vaccination at the 11–12-year-old visit has had on disease incidence. I urge the committee to ensure continued protection against all strains by providing a stronger routine recommendation for MenB, in addition to maintaining the current adolescent recommendations. When you look at the cost of prevention, I hope you also calculate the human toll of this disease and how high the cost of survival is as well.

While NMA ceased active operations in 2022 after 20 years, in large part due the current success of the meningococcal vaccination schedule, the families that were involved in the organization remain passionate about educating others about the importance of preventing this deadly disease. It is our sincere hope that no other family ever has to relive the nightmare of meningitis and navigate the destruction it leaves in its path.

Sincerely,
Lori Buher
Former Secretary, National Meningitis Association (inactive)

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Dear Members of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP),

Thank you for your continued leadership in shaping our national vaccination policies. I am writing to express my strong support for the full recommendation of the Novavax COVID-19 vaccine across all eligible age groups, including children, and to urge the committee to consider how shelf-life limitations are affecting equitable access.

1. Novavax Deserves Full ACIP Recommendation:
Novavax’s protein-based COVID-19 vaccine has demonstrated safety and effectiveness and is a critical option for people who cannot or prefer not to receive mRNA vaccines. Despite FDA authorization, the lack of a full ACIP recommendation undermines access. Pharmacies, clinics, and providers often rely on ACIP guidance to stock and administer vaccines, and many will not carry or offer a vaccine without that endorsement. Expanding ACIP’s recommendation would immediately improve public access and bolster vaccine confidence for a segment of the population that remains under-vaccinated.

2. Pediatric Inclusion Is Essential:
Parents seeking non-mRNA options for their children currently have no viable alternative, leaving a gap in our immunization strategy. Recommending Novavax for pediatric use will not only empower families with choices but also increase protection for children and reduce the burden of transmission in schools and communities.

3. Shelf-Life Barriers Must Be Addressed:
The current shelf-life limitations of Novavax present practical challenges for distribution and result in high levels of waste. If supported by stability data, ACIP should advocate for extending the expiration window. This would make it more feasible for providers to offer Novavax without risking excess inventory loss, especially in rural or lower-demand settings.

Millions of Americans who remain hesitant toward mRNA vaccines are waiting for a fully endorsed, widely available alternative. Novavax can help meet that need, but only if ACIP includes it in the routine vaccine schedule for all eligible ages. Your guidance has enormous influence — not just in what vaccines people receive, but in whether they’re able to receive them at all.

Thank you for considering this request and for your service to public health.

I received Novavax in Fall 2023 and I did not have any adverse reaction and was able to work the next day.

While the mRNA vaccines are also highly effective, some individuals have experienced onerous negative reactions to the mRNA formulations, which they report they do not experience from the Novavax vaccine. These folks are better served by the availability of the Novavax option. title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Vaccine access is personally important to me because I live and work at a university with people who travel from various destinations and can carry various diseases. Additionally, on breaks, I live with my immunocompromised grandmother who is also a cancer patient. I myself am also high-risk for complications from flu, RSV, measles, and COVID, so I rely on regular, timely vaccination to keep myself and my community safe and able to work to make ends meet.

title: Comment from Holt, Megan
comment:
My name is Megan, and I’m a dual degree holder in the sciences; my first degree is a bachelor’s degree in kinesiology, with a focus on exercise physiology and biomechanics with a specialization in adapted physical activity from Michigan State University, and my second degree is a bachelor’s degree in nursing from Creighton University. I am a registered nurse, and have been so for 12 years as of this year; of those 12 years, 8 of them have been spent working in critical care settings. I began my career in critical care by working in long term acute care settings; LTAC is all the fun of ICU with 5 or more patients per shift, and you rarely get to see the same kind of meaningful outcomes as one does in the ICU, which lends itself to burnout-in order to avoid that, I accepted a position as a nurse in the ICU in late November of 2019, not knowing what was coming for all of us.

I began working in the ICU in late January of 2020; I loved it-it was fascinating and challenging, but it was so much fun and I loved the challenges…until covid hit. We had our first covid case on 3/11/20, and it took us 18 days to expand our 16 bed ICU into a 68 bed ICU and run out of ventilators for the *FIRST* time (we ran out of them many, many more times after that.)

We all saw so much death; we had to rotate the bodies in and out of the morgues because we had too many bodies and too little space. We all have patients whose names, faces, stories, dreams, and deaths we still-and will always-carry with us.

Being an ICU RN, I was one of the first people to get vaccinated; my whole family cried with relief when I did get vaccinated. However, we were all worried still, as I am a poor responder to most vaccines-I’ve had to have nearly every vaccine series at least twice over. That’s why when I saw the scientific literature on Novavax, I was thrilled: it’s a vaccine that has fewer side and adverse effects, with a stronger AND longer immune response, and is more like a traditional protein-based vaccine, as opposed to both Pfizer and Moderna’s mRNA based vaccines (and we won’t even get into the whole mess with the J&J vaccine!)

Basically, Novavax is the vaccine against Covid that should be held up as the gold standard against which other covid vaccines are measured. The safety profile of it is excellent, and the previous few years have shown that. As a strong proponent of vaccination, I have received both Moderna and Novavax vaccines (four Moderna doses and three Novavax doses), and I believe that offering multiple fully authorized vaccine platforms benefits public health.
