title: Comment from Kasten, Tim-Oliver
comment:
Subject: Consideration of Vaxart’s Oral Vaccine Solutions for Norovirus and COVID-19
Dear Members of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices,
As you convene to discuss advancements in immunization practices, I urge you to consider the innovative solutions offered by Vaxart for combating norovirus and COVID-19. Vaxart’s oral vaccine platform presents several compelling advantages that align with the goals of enhancing public health and improving vaccine accessibility.
1. Enhanced Immunogenicity and Safety: Vaxart’s oral pill vaccines have demonstrated robust and durable antibody responses in clinical trials. For instance, their norovirus vaccine candidate showed strong immunogenicity in elderly adults, a population often facing reduced immune responses to traditional injected vaccines1. Similarly, their COVID-19 vaccine candidate has progressed to Phase 2b trials, showing promising safety and efficacy data2.
2. Ease of Administration: Oral vaccines eliminate the need for needles, making them more accessible and less intimidating for individuals with needle phobia. This ease of administration can significantly improve vaccination rates, especially in underserved communities and among populations with limited access to healthcare facilities.
3. Mucosal Immunity: Vaxart’s oral vaccines generate potent antibody responses in mucosal tissues, which are critical for combating pathogens that enter through mucosal surfaces1. This unique feature enhances the overall effectiveness of the vaccine in preventing infections.
4. Scalability and Distribution: The oral pill format simplifies storage and distribution logistics, reducing the need for cold chain infrastructure. This can facilitate rapid and widespread deployment, particularly in regions with challenging logistics and infrastructure.
5. Versatility and Future Potential: Vaxart’s platform is not limited to norovirus and COVID-19. It has the potential to be adapted for other infectious diseases, offering a versatile tool in the ongoing fight against global health threats3.
Given these advantages, Vaxart’s oral vaccine solutions represent a significant advancement in immunization technology. I strongly recommend that the committee consider these innovative approaches as part of the broader strategy to enhance public health and combat infectious diseases effectively.
Thank you for your attention to this.

Best regards

Tim Kasten

title: Comment from Saur, Thomas
comment:
To the Members of the CDC Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices,

As a practicing general physician with a strong background in clinical research and immunology, and having formerly served as a Medical Advisor at Novartis in Germany, I would like to offer some reflections on current immunization strategies for airborne viruses.

In 2020, like many of my colleagues, I believed that the induction of neutralizing IgG antibodies through intramuscular vaccination would significantly contribute to curbing the spread and severity of COVID-19. This approach had precedent, as it has long been the cornerstone of influenza vaccination strategies.

However, my perspective began to shift after encountering a study published in The Lancet, comparing the efficacy of a quadrivalent influenza vaccine with a monovalent pill based on the VAAST platform developed by Vaxart. Remarkably, the pill—despite containing only a single influenza A antigen—demonstrated slightly superior effectiveness compared to the licensed quadrivalent vaccine targeting both influenza A and B.

Following that publication, I recall that Dr. Fauci made a favorable statement recognizing the potential of mucosal and T-cell-mediated immunity in enhancing vaccine protection. I assumed this would initiate a broader reevaluation of how we approach immunization for respiratory pathogens, emphasizing the importance of mucosal protection and robust T-cell responses.

But the political focus reamained unchainged on IgG. Iam not adressing the IgG4 shift which may ocur, reducing efficacy.

Unfortunately, from what I can gather in the current agenda, little has changed. The focus still appears to remain on traditional intramuscular vaccines for airbone viruses, rather than embracing and investing in more modern and potentially superior immunization strategies.

I respectfully urge the committee to consider this growing body of evidence and to support the advancement of innovative vaccine platforms that more effectively target the transmission dynamics and immune pathways relevant to airborne viruses.

Sincerely
Thomas Saur, MD

title: Comment from Williams, Erin
comment: I am the one in eleven million who had an anaphylactic response to the mRNA COVID-19 vaccine. The first shot, the pharmacist gave me Benadryl, and I was ok after while. I got the second shot because my spouse has polycystic kidney disease and prediabetes, plus I thought it was my patriotic duty. That mRNA vaccine triggered months and months of outbreaks/revivals for me of Epstein-Barr, shingles, and HSV-1. These outbreaks subsided when the COVID vaccine efficacy did, around the 6-month mark. I submitted data to the database about vaccine adverse reactions. I reported my reactions to my doctor. I even reached out to a friend who helped develop the vaccine I received to see if anyone was researching these rare responses. I was almost universally met with the kind of responses that women have come to expect in healthcare settings: "Are you sure you weren't just nervous and hyperventilating?" I have no history of such. Today, researchers are finally looking into these reactions, which is great. It's too bad that the government couldn't have let the public know that such reactions, although rare, are legitimate; I know it was important to instill confidence in the public about the vaccine, but it should have also empowered doctors to see these reactions as legitimate concerns. This past winter, I finally got COVID for the first time and had no outbreaks of the viruses that I did with the mRNA vaccine, so now I know it was about the vaccine. That made me eager to get Novavax. "Secretary" Kennedy should divest from his "wellness" business interests and stop meddling in the scientific process of bringing more actual solutions to Americans. mRNA holds much promise for many Americans, and that's wonderful, but we need alternatives that countries around the world have seen work, and we definitely don't need someone with antiscience ideas and conflicts of interest limiting our options.

title: Comment from Snook, Chris
comment:
Like millions of other Americans, COVID-19 nearly killed me, and left me with disabilities that I have not fully recovered from years later. Due to an autoimmune response, I am at high risk of dangerous complications both from reinfection and from taking the wrong vaccine. I had had unpleasant reactions to both mRNA vaccines even before my life-threatening autoimmune response to COVID, so I was elated to learn that there was a vaccine available that was safer for people with my risk factors. I went to a pharmacy across the street from a hospital, got a Novavax shot, and waited. Nothing happened. I was safe. The next morning I woke up and felt as good as I had any other morning since getting COVID. I got Novavax again last fall, and again I felt fine after. I didn't even have the superficial symptoms that I had after the mRNA vaccines. At this point, more than two years later, it *might* be safe for me to get an mRNA vaccine, but no doctor who has treated me has been comfortable saying that. There is no test available that can tell me if it would be safe for me to get an mRNA vaccine.

My point in this is not to override the mountains of empirical evidence you have regarding the safety of Novavax in general, but rather to highlight that whatever the averages may be, not every person is average. A vaccine policy tailored only to the average American would be disastrous for many of us who are not. We need a meaningful alternative to mRNA vaccines that slightly different mRNA vaccines can't provide. Please let us have Novavax again this year.

title: Comment from Madden, Kathryn
comment:
My name is Kathryn Madden, and I am submitting this comment to express my deep concern regarding the transparency of recent FDA decision-making processes related to vaccine recommendations, as well as to advocate for timely and equitable access to updated vaccines for the 2025–2026 respiratory virus season. As a concerned citizen who follows public health developments closely and deeply values the role of science in safeguarding community health, I feel compelled to speak out in support of vaccine access, scientific integrity, and public trust.
2. Background
I am specifically commenting on the process and content surrounding the FDA's Vaccines and Related Biological Products Advisory Committee (VRBPAC) meetings, and the broader planning and rollout of COVID-19 and influenza vaccines. I am disturbed by the lack of public transparency in a recent VRBPAC meeting, which occurred behind closed doors, excluded typical expert advisory input, and offered no opportunity for public comment.
3. Analysis
Scientific independence is the cornerstone of vaccine policy. Decisions about vaccine recommendations and approvals must be made with complete integrity, free from political influence. Holding closed meetings without the usual expert panel undermines public confidence and weakens the transparency that has long underpinned the success of our national vaccination programs.
I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with FDA-approved and authorized vaccines—developed using rigorous safety and efficacy standards—saves lives and improves the quality of life for individuals of all ages. This includes mRNA vaccines from Pfizer and Moderna, as well as protein-based vaccines like Novavax. All of these platforms play a critical role in offering choice and maintaining high vaccine uptake across diverse populations.
Looking ahead to the 2025–2026 season, updated COVID-19 vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types must be made available in a timely manner, ideally by July or August, to ensure protection before the start of the academic year and anticipated seasonal virus surges. Annual delays in availability pose a risk to children returning to K-12 schools and others at elevated risk. Likewise, updated influenza vaccines must be rolled out for all age groups without delay in fall 2025.
4. Recommendations
I respectfully urge the FDA and advisory committee members to:
•Recommit to a transparent, public advisory process that includes open meetings, full participation of independent experts, and public comment opportunities;
•Ensure timely availability of updated COVID-19 vaccines (mRNA and non-mRNA platforms) for all age groups no later than August 2025;
•Guarantee widespread access to updated influenza vaccines for all ages in the fall of 2025;
•Speak out publicly in support of the science-based vaccination programs that protect public health and save lives.
5. Conclusion
The public deserves a transparent, scientifically rigorous process for vaccine recommendations—one that includes expert voices and community input. I strongly support routine access to safe and effective vaccines and ask this committee to prioritize both transparency and timely vaccine availability in preparation for the upcoming respiratory virus season. Thank you for your continued dedication to the health and safety of our communities.
Sincerely,
Kathryn Madden

title: Comment from Stailey, Melissa
comment:
As someone who is preparing for brain surgery, I am distraught that Novavax is so difficult to access. At a time when my body is vulnerable, both from the condition requiring surgery and the procedure itself, not having any viable vaccine protection is terrifying. The stress of navigating this reality, while knowing that an option exists but is effectively out of reach, feels like a cruel and unnecessary burden. For patients like me, the inability to access Novavax isn’t just a matter of inconvenience—it’s a life-threatening gap in care.

It is unconscionable to withhold a vaccine that has consistently demonstrated fewer adverse reactions and broad efficacy, especially when it can make the difference between safety and serious harm. Public health policy should prioritize diverse, accessible vaccine options—not just for the general population, but especially for those who are most medically fragile. The failure to make Novavax widely available sends a disturbing message: that the needs of vulnerable patients are secondary. We need better. And we need it now.

Novavax represents a critical option for people with complex health conditions who cannot tolerate mRNA vaccines, and for many immunocompromised individuals, it may be the only viable form of protection against COVID-19. Unlike mRNA-based vaccines, Novavax uses a traditional protein subunit technology that has been widely used in other vaccines for decades. For patients who have experienced serious adverse reactions to mRNA vaccines—such as autoimmune flare-ups, neurological complications, or allergic responses—Novavax offers a safer alternative with a lower risk profile. The stakes are especially high for those of us whose immune systems are already compromised, whether due to chronic illness, immunosuppressive medications, or upcoming surgeries that put us at even greater risk for infection.

title: Comment from Parke, Cameron
comment: The Novavax Covid vaccine, in my opinion, is the best one on the market and available for people in the United States. It does not use the still disputed mRNA method of delivery, but uses a proven technology and method for vaccine delivery. I have received the Moderna mRNA Covid vaccine and the Novavax Covid vaccine over the past 5 years, and Novavax is the one from which I had no side effects. The Moderna mRNA Covid vaccine shots left me achy, feeling sick, and tired for several days afterwards. Novavax was much easier, and one that I tolerated much better. I would not feel comfortable having my children, who are younger than 11, get an mRNA vaccine, when the technology is so new, and we don't yet know the long-term effects of annual mRNA vaccinations. But, I would feel comfortable with my children getting the Novavax Covid vaccine when they are eligible--I believe at age 12, since the technology has a much longer track record. Contrary to perceived popular opinion, Covid continues to circulate and constitute a major public health problem in the US. It is one thing if someone gets Covid and then has life-long immunity. However, that is clearly not the case--the "immunity," or protection conferred from prior infections--is very short-term, so people are highly susceptible to getting multiple infections over the course of a few years. Covid is not simply a respiratory illness, but can be better described as a vascular disease that can spread and cause severe damage throughout the body. Covid has been shown to cause heart attacks, strokes, blood clots, neurological problems, brain damage, etc. Even more problematically, is that an ever-increasing number of people have been suffering from Long Covid, which has been tied to people suffering from multiple infections. The damage caused by Covid is cumulative--so the more people get infected, their chances of getting Long Covid increases with each subsequent infection. Likewise, Covid infections have been shown to cause immune system damage, weakness, and dysregulation, and has been shown to cause T-cell dysregulation and early death. In short, Covid infections increase the aging process in people. It is NOT good to get Covid, and it is NOT good to get re-infections of Covid. Allowing Covid to circulate unimpeded is a horrific idea and policy being implemented by the federal government, and it will only result in more pre-mature deaths and people being sick more frequently and ever-increasing cases of Long Covid and debilitating disabilities. Covid spreads via aerosols, and primarily in indoor spaces that lack adequate ventilation and clean air. Covid spread could be limited by a multi-pronged public health strategy--increase ventilation in buildings where Covid spread is most likely to occur (like schools, colleges, doctor's offices, hospitals, and other crowded indoor places), make widespread use of HEPA filters to help clean indoor air, encourage masking in certain situations, pour money and resources into finding/making antivirals that would safely help combat Covid when people get infected, promote universal paid sick leave for workers to encourage people to stay home when sick, and use effective and safe Covid vaccines to help prevent people from getting sick with Covid and to help prevent them from suffering the worst case scenarios when they do get infected with Covid. title: Comment from tran, a
comment:
To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.Sincerely,

Expand Novavax Authorization to Include Pediatric Use (6 months - 12 years)
Pediatric protection remains a major gap. Parents should have the option to choose a non-mRNA vaccine for their children, especially for those with prior adverse events or concerns about mRNA technology. Novavax has already been shown to be safe and immunogenic in this age group, and the pediatric dose is the same as the adult dose, simplifying rollout.

Extend Shelf Life of Single-Dose Novavax Vials
Current policy only allows a 3-month shelf life, which is unnecessarily short compared to other global regulatory practices. A 6-month (or longer) shelf life would reduce waste, improve planning for fall/winter campaigns, and increase provider willingness to stock Novavax doses.

Ensure Updated COVID Vaccines Are Available by Late Summer 2025
COVID transmission and hospitalizations consistently begin rising in early fall. Delaying vaccine availability until mid- or late fall is too late. To maximize public health protection, especially for high-risk groups and children returning to school, updated vaccines, including Novavax, must be authorized and distributed before the end of summer.

In short, it is vital that ACIP act now to advocate for timely, equitable access to safe, effective COVID-19 vaccines. Novavax is a crucial option in this effort, and further delays are unacceptable. Thank you for considering these comments.

Respectfully,
Donna M. Herinya

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Dear CDC ACIP committee members,

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

title: Comment from Greenman, Bethany
comment: The Novavax vaccine offers better protection against COVID-19 than its mRNA counterparts, as it targets a wider range of variants. comment:

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Docket No. CDC-2025-0017-0001

I am a medically high-risk individual calling for Novavax's BLA approval and its expansion for pediatric use. I rely on vaccination one of many vital layers to keep myself safe from reinfection, which can be devastating for those with my condition. On top of all of this, I agonized about whether or not get vaccinated after that- as NPI mitigations disappeared, and I had to weigh the risks of reinfection with the risks of the vaccines, as I heard many accounts of those in the Long Covid community who experienced relapse of their Long Covid symptoms upon vaccination. Many in the Long Covid and chronic illness community were opting for this vaccine as their only option. In the midst of a relapse of my Long Covid symptoms in the fall of 2022, I took my first Novavax vaccine and not only did it provide much-needed protection, but it actually calmed my Long Covid symptoms. Since then, I have only opted for Novavax boosters- but now this invaluable option might be taken away.

If Novavax is no longer an option, many will no longer be able to, or choose to be, vaccinated, as the mRNA vaccine led to adverse side effects for them. No one should have to weigh whether or not protection from Covid reinfection is worth sustaining side effects from mRNA vaccines that may set back their recovery. We need freedom of choice to choose which medicines to put in our bodies and continued access to Covid vaccines, as those of us who are medically high-risk continue to need protection from this dangerous virus.

title: Comment from Valenzuela, Ananda
comment:
Docket: CDC-2025-0017-0001

April 9, 2025

Public Comment for the April 15-16, 2025 meeting of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices

To the CDC ACIP committee:

I have no conflicts of interest to disclose. As a small business owner and community member, I care deeply about keeping everyone safe and healthy, So I would like to echo the wisdom of Kaitlin Sundling, MD, PhD: I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

title: Comment from Felton-Lui, Kaycee
comment:
To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to emphatically support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including the continuation of regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. Novax has been an essential tool for me and is the only COVID vaccine I've been able to access that does not come with a day or two of challenging side effects. Novavax allows me and others to protect ourselves from COVID with minimal interruption to our lives.

All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

Thank you for your consideration and efforts to ensure continued vaccine access.

Sincerely,

K. Felton-Lui

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Approve Novavax. Keep the mRNA COVID-19 vaccines in rotation, too. Please do not take any of our vaccines out of availability to the public, nor limit access. This will kill and disable people, especially very young children.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

Vaccines have saved the lives of 154 million children in the past 50 years, according to this prominent Nature study (https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-025-00862-1). Vaccines provide protection against infectious diseases like measles and influenza, but also increasingly against cancer; the HPV vaccine's adoption have dropped the deaths by cervical cancer by 75% for women under 25. mRNA vaccines show promise to protect us against other cancers, like pancreatic cancer ( https://www.nih.gov/news-events/nih-research-matters/mrna-vaccine-treat-pancreatic-cancer ).

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

Getting more specific:
1. I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

2. We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well. Novavax in particular is crucial because it causes less side effects for people with a variety of preexisting conditions.

3. I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

4. Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Its symptoms are wide-ranging and can include crushing fatigue, cognitive dysfunction (“brain fog”), shortness of breath, chest pain, muscle and joint pain, dizziness, gastrointestinal issues, and neurological disturbances. title: Comment from Bass, Jason
comment:
I am writing to express my strong support for the full Biologics License Application (BLA) approval for Novavax’s COVID-19 vaccine and to urge clarity and correction regarding recent public misinformation on vaccine science, specifically comments made by Robert F. Kennedy Jr., who falsely claimed that single-antigen vaccines do not work.

Novavax’s NVX-CoV2373 has demonstrated robust efficacy, a favorable safety profile, and unique benefits compared to existing COVID-19 vaccines. The protein-subunit platform used by Novavax—combined with its Matrix-M adjuvant—offers several compelling advantages:

Efficacy: Clinical trials have consistently shown NVX-CoV2373 provides strong protection against COVID-19, including across multiple variants.

Safety: The vaccine has a well-documented safety profile, with lower rates of reactogenicity in some populations, making it suitable for individuals who are hesitant or unable to take mRNA-based vaccines.

Alternative for the Vaccine-Hesitant: The Novavax vaccine offers a non-mRNA option for those seeking alternatives to newer technologies, potentially increasing overall vaccination rates.

The availability of diverse vaccine platforms strengthens our national response to current and future pandemics. Granting full BLA approval is a critical step in recognizing Novavax as a foundational piece of the COVID-19 immunization landscape.

Recently, Robert F. Kennedy Jr. falsely claimed that single-antigen vaccines do not work, a statement that is both scientifically inaccurate and publicly irresponsible.

Single-antigen vaccines have a long, proven history. Vaccines such as hepatitis B, HPV, and meningococcal B are based on single antigens and have been highly effective at preventing severe illness and saving lives.

Efficacy depends on design, not antigen count. A vaccine’s effectiveness lies in the relevance and immunogenicity of the antigen, not in how many are used. For certain pathogens, one well-targeted antigen is all that’s needed to generate strong, durable immunity.

His claim contradicts decades of vaccine science. The idea that single-antigen vaccines “don’t work” undermines public understanding of immunology and falsely discredits the success of numerous licensed and widely used vaccines.

Making False statements puts lives at risk. In a time when vaccine confidence is already fragile, misleading the public about the fundamentals of vaccine science has real-world consequences, including decreased uptake and resurgence of preventable diseases.

By propagating such false claims, Mr. Kennedy contributes to a dangerous erosion of public trust in vaccines and public health institutions.

We are at a pivotal moment where science, public trust, and global health policy intersect. The FDA has a duty to continue making decisions grounded in rigorous data and the public good. Approving Novavax’s BLA and publicly addressing dangerous misinformation are essential steps in reinforcing evidence-based health policy.

Thank you for your continued leadership and your commitment to protecting public health through sound scientific decision-making.

Sincerely,
Jason Bass

title: Comment from Fasi, Joseph
comment:
To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,
Joseph M Fasi

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination

title: Comment from Public Health - Seattle & King County, Immunization Branch
comment:
ACIP has been and remains an essential partner to public health professionals and the communities we serve, in large part due to its openness, transparency, and adherence to the highest scientific standards. As HHS and ACIP undergo changes and reevaluate past practices and policies, we urge you to maintain the foundations of good governance and scientific integrity that have been the hallmarks of ACIP. This includes open meetings, selecting committee members without conflicts of interest, welcoming and considering input from the public, and basing recommendations on the best available science. When these standards are met, we can assure our partners and the public that ACIP recommendations reflect a broad consensus among relevant experts, not cherry-picked studies or the idiosyncrasies or private interests of any one member.

Open ACIP and VRBPAC meetings demonstrate the thoughtful and informed discussions about scientific evidence that precede ACIP recommendations. And because participants in ACIP are thoroughly vetted and adhere to strict rules for conflict of interest, we can assure our stakeholders that the decisions do not reflect any ulterior motives. ACIP members who have played any role in creating or testing a vaccine disclose that history, but their insights into the specifics of that vaccine are valuable and should not be excluded. Current rules require that they divest any financial stakes in those vaccines, ensuring that science, not personal bias, drives the discussion. ACIP has been rigorous about excluding improper conflicts of interest and we urge you to continue doing so.

The growing measles outbreaks in our nation (712 confirmed cases, 2 confirmed deaths, and one death under investigation as of April 11) underscore the cost of vaccine misinformation, mistrust, and under vaccination: preventable illnesses and deaths. Open, transparent decision-making and evidence-based recommendations build trust.

Furthermore, ACIP decisions directly impact the public’s ability to access vaccines. ACIP immunization recommendations that have been approved by the CDC Director and appear on CDC immunization schedules generally must be covered by applicable health insurance plans, thereby removing the cost burden of vaccines on the public. We ask that ACIP continue to recommend the well-tested and safe vaccines currently included on the childhood and adult immunization schedules, including influenza, COVID-19, and MMR, and uphold the responsibility of promoting vaccine access. We urge you not to let political whim override the evidence-based decisions of the past or the integrity of decision-making processes going forward.

We thank current and past ACIP members for their service, and the time and energy they have given to the nation and the public health community.
-Immunization Branch, Public Health – Seattle & King County

title: Comment from Jones, Alexandra
comment:
To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5 percent of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47 percent of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Alexandra Jones

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,
a concerned citizen of these United States.

P.S. How's THAT for a form letter???

Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,
Larisa

title: Comment from American Lung Association
comment:
We, the undersigned medical, public health and patient organizations, offer the following comments to the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP):

As our nation faces ongoing public health threats, it is critical that we have the expertise needed to prevent and respond to disease outbreaks. By providing timely, up-to-date, evidence-based immunization recommendations, ACIP plays a key role in protecting public health. Vaccines are a lifesaving tool and the best way to protect individuals in the U.S. from numerous serious diseases. ACIP-recommended vaccines are rigorously tested and reviewed before recommendation and can prevent disease outbreaks before they start.

Currently, the measles outbreak that began in the Southwest United States is spreading to other parts of the country. As of April 3, there have been 607 confirmed cases of measles in 22 states, 74 people have been hospitalized, and experts are concerned that this could represent a significant undercount. Evidence appears to indicate that this outbreak is not under control, and is spreading; sadly, two people have already died with a third death under investigation. This outbreak is occurring on the heels of the worst flu season in over a decade. There have been 168 pediatric deaths, and at least 45 million illnesses, 580,000 hospitalizations, and 25,000 deaths from flu so far this season. And whooping cough incidence remains elevated after seeing more cases in 2024 than in over a decade. , Outbreak responses require prompt direction and up-to-date expertise.

To combat these threats, it is crucial that ACIP continue to hold regular meetings and provide timely, evidence-based immunization recommendations to inform public health officials, providers, and patients. Local, state, and federal public health experts rely on guidance from ACIP during outbreaks and in order to prevent future outbreaks. Our organizations appreciate ACIP’s ongoing commitment to public health and look forward to working with you to help keep the country safe.

Thank you for the opportunity to provide comments.

Sincerely,
American Lung Association
SAFE Communities Coalition
Vaccinate Your Family
