title: Comment from Jersey, Jester
comment:
I am writing in support of the ACIP's planned vote to recommend that GSK's MenABCWY vaccine may be administered when both MenACWY and MenB are indicated during the same visit.

I have seen the devastation caused by meningitis B, an easily preventable yet destructive disease, robbing life and health from the youngest and most promising lives in our society. Families shouldn't have to grieve the loss of a loved one simply because the right vaccine policy wasn't in place that would support a scientifically sound vaccine. No family has to experience this.

We have already seen the devastation brought on by the early years of the COVID pandemic five years ago. We have also seen the resilience and assistance young people gave when the first set of COVID vaccines were approved for those 16 years of age and older, giving our young people an opportunity to help fight the pandemic along with older peers. Many of these young adults are continuing to contribute to our society, yet can be affected by a disease that doesn't get as much awareness like COVID did. Nobody should become a victim to a disease after helping our nation fight off another disease solely because we don't want to invest in our youth.

By not voting to recommend GSK's MenABCWY vaccine now at a time when our health infrastructure has already been drastically weakened by the pandemic is like a disaster waiting to happen. Vaccine rates for most vaccine-preventable diseases have already been low even prior to the current measles outbreak and by not approving a vaccine that has already passed FDA scrutiny, vaccine rates will continue to decrease while disease will only increase. National health infrastructure to provide good health to our young citizens and the quality of health for many will only decline if safe vaccines like this one are not approved.

While Serogroup B meningitis only accounts for a portion of the different serogroups, it accounts for a third of nationwide cases, and is by far the most prevalent in young adults, who populate our nation's fine learning institutes, such as high schools and colleges. These are also only a portion of the entirety of young adults that we know are affected- there are also those who may not be in an educational institution or who have easy access to a health facility that can accurately diagnose the disease or may misdiagnose a Serogroup B meningitis case as something else.

It does not make sense for one of the most widespread and devastating serogroups of meningitis to have confusing language around their recommendation, confusion on whether to get them or not by the individual, and no uniform practice among our nation's health providers. Our young people deserve better from our national health leaders. They count on us to protect them from threats that they cannot fight on their own.

Last October, when I spoke at the last ACIP meeting, it coincided as the same month as World Meningitis Day. When I provided my public comment, I not only advocated for a vaccine that specifically focused on Serogroup B meningitis, not just Serogroups A, C, W, and Y, but that the CDC also clarify the confusing language around recommendations so that there's more consensus among health personnel. Even when individuals know they may be susceptible to Serogroup B meningitis, and manage to get into their health practitioner's office in time, they can still be met with the obstacle of confusion. The last thing we need while trying to save young lives is a combination of a lack of helpful policy, confusing recommendations and mixed messaging from their health providers.

The physicians who administer the series of meningitis vaccines should also in turn be knowledgeable about what options to offer their patients based on sound science and the CDC's recommendation. As patients, members of their family and the community, we all look up to our local health providers to know what to do and what to give to us during our time of medical need. If confusing recommendations continue to be promoted, we can have the best vaccines with high efficacy rates, but if we cannot get them to the people who need them the most, they will be rendered useless. Recommendations that we provide are just as important as the tools we have to fight vaccine-preventable disease. Please remember that the whole country is watching ACIP's recommendation, and I want to advocate on behalf of those affected by this vaccine-preventable disease so that no one has to grieve the loss of a child and a friend.

I recommend that the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices continue to refine recommendations so that the public can understand the recommendations the CDC makes from the outcome of this meeting, approve GSK's MenABCWY vaccine so that more lives can be saved, and take into account the pro-vaccine comments that support both GSK's MenABCWY vaccine and the other safe vaccines being considered during this current ACIP meeting. Thank you.

title: Comment from National Minority Quality Forum
comment:
The National Minority Quality Forum (NMQF) is a 501(c)(3) not-for-profit research and advocacy organization based in Washington, DC. The mission of NMQF is to reduce patient risk by assuring optimal care for all. Our vision is an American health services research, delivery and financing system whose operating principle is to reduce patient risk for amenable morbidity and mortality while improving quality of life. NMQF’s forward-looking approach to population health operates at the nexus of the humanity-centric imperatives of the physical laws and the social contract.

The National Minority Quality Forum is submitting this public comment for consideration during the April 15-16, 2025, meeting of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP). Specifically, NMQF supports the development of recommendations that will help ensure that adults have timely and affordable access to technologies that prevent amenable morbidity and mortality associated with Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV).

title: Comment from Ruiz, Christina
comment:
To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,

Christina Ruiz

title: Comment from MAIER, LESLIE
comment:
Dear Members of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP):
Many of you may be familiar with my son Chris’ story about his short and tragic battle with meningococcal disease. While Chris passed away 20 years ago, as his mother, it still feels like yesterday. I share my son’s story with you again as a reminder of the lasting impact meningococcal disease has on families, and to implore the committee to put forth the strongest recommendations for meningococcal vaccination – routine immunization against all strains at the 16-year visit, as well as maintaining the current adolescent recommendations.
Meningitis may be considered a rare disease, but it does not discriminate. One of the things that makes this disease so scary is that it can happen to anyone and kill within a matter of hours, regardless of how healthy you are. That’s what happened to my son, Chris. He went from a healthy 17-year-old athlete to being gone in less than 24 hours. The terrifying aspect of meningococcal disease is the initial symptoms are often confused with the flu or other conditions and it can take over the body so quickly that even with appropriate treatment, it often leads to irreversible health consequences or death. Chris had a headache at school but went to soccer practice that night and was sent home early by the coach. He had a fever and couldn’t get warm, vomiting during the night. The next morning, while preparing to go to the doctor, Chris couldn’t feel his feet and was suddenly unresponsive. Paramedics rushed him to the hospital, where they first thought he may be experiencing a potential drug overdose given that he was a teenager. Then they decided he had cardiac issues. If the paramedics hadn’t demanded a spinal tap, we would never have known that it was meningococcal disease that took his life.
We had never even heard of meningococcal disease before Chris passed away and were devastated. We found out later that there was a recommended vaccine that could have possibly saved our son’s life. No parent should ever be faced with having to remove their child from life support due to a disease that is preventable. I vowed then to do all I could to ensure no other parent would have to endure the same pain our family experienced.
While the incidence of meningococcal disease has declined as the result of available vaccines and the adolescent immunization program, we know the disease remains in circulation due to recent outbreaks. Also, thanks to the vaccine’s success, many healthcare providers today have not had direct experience diagnosing and treating meningococcal meningitis. This, coupled with the virus being highly unpredictable, poses a serious risk for a return of increased disease, as well as misdiagnosed cases, if strong protection is not maintained.
As I said before, meningitis does not discriminate, and there is no way to predict who meningococcal disease will strike. This is why I also support not only keeping the current schedule, but also issuing a stronger recommendation for the MenB vaccine.
During my time as former president of the National Meningitis Association (operations ceased in 2022), we worked closely with public health and strongly supported the ACIP deliberation process to educate parents and young people about CDC recommendations. This work was driven largely by parents and families who intimately knew the devastating impact of meningococcal disease and did not want other families to meet the same fate.
It is my sincere hope that the committee will take the strongest approach to preventing more families from experiencing the pain and loss associated with this disease.
Sincerely,
Leslie Maier
Past-President
National Meningitis Association (inactive)

title: Comment from O'Neill, Jason
comment:
Docket: CDC-2025-0017-0001

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. (https://www.nbcnews.com/health/health-news/rfk-jr-vaccines-children-weakening-system-hhs-budget-cuts-rcna199188) We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

title: Comment from National Consumers League
comment:
The National Consumers League (NCL) is the nation’s pioneering consumer organization, with 125 years of history advocating and advancing consumer protections in all areas, including but not limited to child labor, food and nutrition, fraud, live event ticketing fairness and health care. NCL is dedicated to protection and education for consumers and workers since 1899. NCL’s first General Secretary, Florence Kelley, lost 5 of her 7 siblings to childhood illnesses, including Diphtheria. Today, NCL provides government, businesses and other organizations with the consumer’s perspective on key issues.

Since the NCL’s founding in 1899, NCL has worked to increase public access to and confidence in immunization and other forms of preventive health measures. It is in continuing to fulfill that work that we appreciate the opportunity to submit these comments to the Committee. Please see the attached file for our full written comments to the Advisory Committee for the April 15 - 16, 2025 meeting.

comment:

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely, Liz Igo

S. Phonese

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,

title: Comment from Bein, Lainey
comment:
I am writing to express my strong support for the scientific independence and transparency of this committee, including the ongoing conduct of open meetings with advisors who represent medical and public health professionals grounded in science, along with patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendations must take place free from any political influence. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting was held privately, without the chance for public input, and without the participation of the usual expert advisors.

I agree with the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective vaccines approved and authorized by the FDA against a wide range of pathogens saves lives for both children and adults while enhancing our quality of life. Public health is under serious threat from multiple fronts, including mass firings of federal public health employees and reductions in research and program funding. In the name of combating censorship, funding for research on vaccine hesitancy has been reduced. With a growing measles outbreak occurring in several US communities—particularly in rural and underserved areas where MMR vaccination rates are significantly below the necessary levels for herd immunity—the funding for the highly effective and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut.

I strongly encourage members of this committee to advocate at every chance for our safe and effective vaccines, and I invite anyone reading these remarks to contact their elected representatives to emphasize that access to vaccines is crucial for public health and disease prevention. Public health will suffer if safe and effective vaccines are not made available through health insurance or free programs for those who are uninsured or underinsured. If public vaccination initiatives are eliminated for political motivations, we all bear the consequences.

I would like to share some particular thoughts regarding additional topics for the agenda of this meeting. For the voting matters, I encourage you to think about streamlining the RSV vaccine guidelines to permit vaccination based solely on age rather than limiting it to specific risk groups for individuals aged 50 to 74. Implementing a straightforward age-based recommendation could reduce the obstacles to vaccination for high-risk populations.

Thank you to this committee for your continued work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely
L.B.

Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. Many people the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you for your time and attention to this comment.

To the CDC ACIP committee:

I want to express my strong support to this committee for continued scientific independence and transparency. I ask that these meetings continue to be held openly and with the ability for the public to make comments in support of or against specific recommendations as this is a foundation of a strong democracy. Decisions around vaccine recommendations should not be issues discussed behind closed doors and should not be subject to political interference as political interference mirrors the actions of authoritarian states. Advisors and those on this committee need to be committed to doing the right thing, not the politically favorable thing.

I am in strong support of vaccines and their ability to prime the immune system to resist infection at many different ages. They are an amazing technology, built on the shoulders of people who were not afraid to ask questions and conduct scientific testing in order to protect large populations. Vaccines save lives, period. We can look at the recent measles outbreaks among unvaccinated communities as an example of the amazing ability of vaccines to stop illness and we should take very seriously the health of other people. I know that many people are afraid or skeptical of vaccines due to misinformation and misattribution, such as when people link vaccines to autism rather than thinking about other confounds. It's easier to blame something tangible like a vaccine rather than face a myriad of unknown variables that added up to produce a result around the same time. This fear of public backlash is no reason to back down from doing the right thing. Education reduces fear.

Please speak out in support of vaccination. Please commit to advocate for free/low-cost vaccines for everyone.

I would also like to express my support for updated COVID-19 and influenza vaccinations for this year. I would like to see Novavax continue to be made available as I switched from Pfizer/Moderna two years ago and have had a much easier time being vaccinated with Novavax over the other two. This study indicates that being unvaccinated for COVID-19 shows a greatly increased risk of heart attack and other serious cardiovascular outcomes post COVID-19 infection compared to a vaccinated cohort: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-46497-0

Please consider carefully and support any new and effective COVID-19 vaccine technology that might emerge into the market. I know there are several nasal vaccine candidates that can prime the cells in the nostrils to protect someone against infection altogether which is a much greater outcome than just preventing severe outcomes.

title: Comment from F, Reed
comment:
I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

title: Comment from Carole, Gina
comment:
I’m submitting my comment to strongly urge the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) to
preserve full public access to Novavax in the U.S. COVID-19 vaccination program. Novavax is currently the
only authorized non-mRNA COVID-19 vaccine in the United States. If ACIP removes or weakens its
recommendation, even indirectly, it may be excluded from the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program, lose
preventive care coverage under the Affordable Care Act (ACA), and disappear from pharmacies and clinics
nationwide. These are not abstract consequences. This would functionally eliminate access to the only
remaining non-mRNA vaccine for the American public.

This is not merely about preference. For many patients including myself, Novavax represents the only
viable, non-mRNA vaccine option after injury from mRNA platforms. Removing or deprioritizing Novavax
during the April 2025 review would not only deny access to a scientifically distinct and safer alternative—
it would also deepen the medical abandonment of vaccine-injured individuals and disregard serious,
emerging evidence about the long-term risks of spike persistence, intracellular antigen production, and
systemic inflammation. ACIP has an obligation to protect platform diversity, safety, and trust. That
responsibility must include Novavax.

Novavax’s Broad Public Health Value
Novavax offers several critical broad public health advantages that extend beyond any single patient
group. Unlike mRNA vaccines, it uses a traditional protein subunit platform with a Matrix-M adjuvant,
requiring no intracellular spike production or genetic delivery. This reduces biological risk, simplifies
storage and distribution (standard refrigeration rather than ultra-cold storage), and builds trust across
populations with diverse medical and immunological profiles.
Data show that Novavax induces strong neutralizing antibodies and durable T-cell responses across SARS-
CoV-2 variants. Its more stable, predictable formulation allows for use in public health systems without
specialized infrastructure. Maintaining platform diversity ensures national vaccine resilience—avoiding
overreliance on a single technology, particularly as scientific understanding evolves.

Practical Benefits and Reduced Side Effects
Novavax has also been shown to have a milder side effect profile than mRNA vaccines. Patients report
fewer post-vaccination symptoms like fever, myalgia, and fatigue. This difference matters: in both trial
and real-world settings, Novavax is consistently described as less disruptive and better tolerated,
particularly by individuals with heightened immune sensitivity or chronic health conditions.
These attributes make Novavax more acceptable to a wide range of patients, including those hesitant
about newer technologies. It also strengthens population-level vaccine confidence and uptake by
providing a viable choice to those with medical, scientific, or personal concerns about mRNA platforms.

Essential for High-Risk and Medically Complex Populations
Many people with chronic illnesses, especially Long COVID, ME/CFS, mast cell activation, autoimmune
disease, and dysautonomia, report adverse responses to mRNA vaccines or are advised against them.
For these patients, Novavax provides a safer alternative due to its non-replicating, non-intracellular
mechanism of action. Its distinct delivery method avoids key triggers known to exacerbate immune
dysregulation, cytokine flares, or autonomic instability.

title: Comment from Riccio, Alexander
comment:
I wish I had time to type up a full comment.

Instead, I'd like to plead desperately but briefly to authorize novavax. It's the only vaccine that truly gives me no side effects, and the superior immunity it provides is one of the few things that's kept me healthy.

I also implore you to incorporate the costs and risks of post COVID health issues into your modeling, ALONG with the costs of missed opportunities and missed work from infections. When those are omitted, the cost benefit analysis is severely misleading, and harms those of us who are young enough to largely avoid hospitalization for acute COVID.

When I last had COVID, not only was I bedridden with a fever for a full week, but I spent much of the following month and a half unable to function with a mixture of terrifying cardiovascular and neurological issues... Issues which have never been factored into prior cost benefit analysis by ACIP! Indeed I recall the fall meeting including a mention that the committee felt unable to properly model these types of costs, and thus had not even attempted to do so. I now have many friends under 40 who have struggled with similar health issues, with a few fully disabled, none captured in prior cost benefit analysis. There are essentially no reliable and effective treatments for us. Thousands of papers and journal reviews at this point show that the protection against infection provided by high neutralizing titers is just as important (for preventing medium term and long term complications) as it is to provide the protection of reduced severity of infection. Novavax appears to exceed the other options in this respect. Given the possibility that novavax provokes a less immunotolerant response, it also appears to be a good candidate for the CDC to approve in advance the repeat administration at intervals as frequent as 2-4 months *for all age groups* in order to provide the sterilizing immunity that we need to avoid the longer term complications. I cannot highlight that last point enough - when I had my temporarily disabling infection, it had been 13 months since the CDC had approved a booster for my age group. I was left to fall by the wayside by the decisions of federal officials who believed the risk of my suffering wasn't worth the few hundred bucks (max) that it would have cost to boost me on an interval more concordant with my exposure. I wish to further support that with a mention of the now multi-year history of COVID surging to exceptional prevalence (as much as 5-10%!) multiple times per year, in spring, summer, and fall, in addition to the winter, as a further implorment to not apply the once-a-year-in-late-fall/winter a-la-flu schedule when fully approving Novavax. When I had my temporarily disabling infection, it was in late fall, a few weeks before the fall/winter booster was finally approved for my age group. There is no fair way to protect us without protecting us consistently throughout the year.

I wish to finally reiterate that the protection against infection provided by high titers that fade as quickly as two to four months is a protection that is worth planning to approve as a precaution, and not waiting until the obviously predictable post market surveillance data arrives in January. The pattern has repeated every year now for four years: highly sterilizing immunity decays for all starting at two months, the elderly drastically lose protection against hospitalization (but not necessarily death) starting at four months, and protection against moderate to minor exposures begins to decay severely in the following months for all.

The only policy that avoids the compounding health and economic costs of ongoing spread of COVID is a policy that provides all layers of protection against infection with the full throated backing of our federal agencies.

You must err on the side of caution and approve novavax immediately.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
To ACIP regarding Novavax authorization:
I have worked as a nurse in a hospital unit since 2004. I got my only covid infection in August of 2022 from a coworker. I developed POTS symptoms on day 9 of my otherwise very mild covid infection - mainly my heart rate doubled every time I stood up requiring me to miss work for several days. My POTS symptoms were slowing improving in November of 2022 and I was back at work as another covid wave was starting. I wear a respirator at work, but those aren't perfect, and sometimes I work 12.5 hour shifts without a lunch or meal break. So I occasionally need to take my mask off to eat and drink. I thought the best way to protect my health and minimize my chance of getting another covid infection would be to get a yearly covid booster 3 months after my infection (Nov 2022), as was recommended by the CDC. Seeing rising rates in the patient population and in my coworkers, I got an mRNA booster in late November 2022. My POTS symptoms worsened significantly starting the day after the mRNA booster. I was forced to go off work for several weeks, followed by months of modified/light duty at the hospital (not in direct patient care as I usually am). By early 2024, I was back on full duty at my hospital nursing job but it was a long road to recovery requiring many doctor visits and two cardiac medications. I have since discovered research that my situation is not unique - for patients with POTS and hypermobility, which I also have, mRNA boosters are associated with worsening POTS symptoms. See the journal article below.

In researching the Novavax vaccine, I was not seeing any correlation to worsening POTS. I received a Novavax booster in December 2024 and had no side effects, and no worsening of my POTS symptoms. Since I continue to work in a hospital, I believe it best protects me from reinfection or if reinfection occurs, reduces my chance of severe disease.

Please authorize Novavax vaccines for the coming year so I can continue to protect my health as a working nurse.

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38176772/
Heart Rhythm
. 2024 Jan;21(1):74-81. doi: 10.1016/j.hrthm.2023.09.012. Epub 2023 Nov 11.
Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome after COVID-19 vaccination
Debbie Lin Teodorescu 1, Anxhela Kote 1, Jewel N Reaso 1, Carine Rosenberg 1, Xiao Liu 1, Alan C Kwan 1, Susan Cheng 1, Peng-Sheng Chen 2

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Hello, I am an immunocompromised and disabled person living with Long COVID that I contracted in 2023, and I am urging the CDC to approve the use to Novavax and its continuous production and updating. title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

title: Comment from O'Dell, Noelle
comment:
Childhood vaccination yields $44 in savings per $1 spent, according to Johns Hopkins University.
The Children’s Hospital of Philadelphia published an accessible piece titled "If vaccines work, why do unvaccinated people present a risk to those who have been vaccinated?" This article helps explain the concept of community (herd) immunity, a concept unfamiliar to many. People often underestimate the seriousness of vaccine-preventable diseases and overestimate the likelihood or severity of side effects. They also may not understand how unvaccinated individuals pose real dangers to newborns, people with certain medical conditions, or those who do not develop an adequate immune response to vaccines.
While much anti-vaccine rhetoric is fueled by disinformation, spread by those who benefit from political divisiveness or are consumed by rampant individualism, many individuals simply lack understanding. They may not realize how personal choices regarding vaccination can endanger others and potentially spark public health crises. For this reason, to prevent more needless suffering and death of innocent children and to forestall widespread public health crises, you must not only fully fund programs such as Vaccines for Children, but also ensure adequate funding for local health departments to disseminate correct information and education through schools, religious institutions and other entities that still enjoy public trust.

title: Comment from C., T.
comment:
My brother and I caught chicken pox at the pediatrician's office.

We were in the waiting room with our mom and as we were called in, another mom and kid came out, and that kid had chicken pox all over him.

A couple days later and both my brother and I had it. My brother must have gotten a whopping dose from that kid or he's a little more susceptible to it because I got a small rash and he got pox all over him. I remember him soaking in the tub, being probably about 3 or 4 years old, and entirely covered. He had pox in his mouth and on his tongue. No amount of calamine lotion was helping. He was crying so hard it was difficult to see.

And of course, I got better fast, so grandma took me to the circus. My brother took a long time to get better so no circus for him.

Fast forward a few decades. In his 40s (too young for Shingrix), my brother got shingles (after a bout of Covid) and it took more than a month for him to recover from how bad that was.

No shingles for me, and I made it to 50+ so I got the shingles vaccine. Hopefully no future shingles either.

These days, of course, we have the chicken pox vaccine, so hopefully a lot of people won't get chicken pox and won't get future shingles either.

And why is this comment full of a chicken pox anecdote (n of 2) when I really want to mention the Covid vaccines, and in particular, Novavax.

Well, first, because it took 40+ years before my brother came down with shingles after his chicken pox infection and we know that chicken pox (actually a herpes virus, not a real pox) hides out in our nerve ganglia, waiting for us to get run down and then show up again.

It is looking pretty likely that Sars-CoV-2 may be playing a similar game as papers are piling up showing that not everyone is clearing the virus entirely.

Which means that vaccine here and now is likely to play a really important role in possibly helping people in that regard -- either to prevent infection in the first place, reduce the severity of the acute onset, and even to possibly help the body clear the virus so that they may not have downstream sequelea. We're 5ish years in on this pandemic. Covid is still novel in that we don't know what happens in 10, 20, 30, 40+ years. And we know lots of pathogens like to rear their ugly heads a long ways down the road. Like HIV turning into AIDS. Like the immune system having to expend more effort every year to suppress Cytomegalovirus infection, that once we get, we never clear.

So it is absolutely of the most importance that we have access to Covid vaccines.

Novavax is important because it is a protein vaccine, so it works for a lot of people who can't take the mRNAs. It also appears to have a lot fewer side effects (n of 1, the mRNAs made me terribly unwell, Novavax made me a little tired for a couple hours, I'm going to want Novavax each year rather than losing a week of work with the mRNA side effects).

So Novavax needs to be available. (Please move it from an emergency use authorization to a full biologic license application approval, thank you!)

And the under 12-year olds would do very well to have it available to them too. The safety data has been accrued for that.

We also need to have the Covid vaccines available at least twice a year. We mostly only get Chicken Pox once in our lives, but Covid likes to mutate and come around again with more variants. Most people I know are getting Covid repeatedly. Some once a year, a few more than once in a year. We had a late summer-early fall wave and then a late fall-winter wave of Covid this year. In between, it never really goes away. Getting sick twice year is a lot of disruption. (No going to the circus!) And each infection causes damage.

Novavax is also going to need a longer expiration date. Part of the trouble has been sourcing Novavax. I'm well read on the research and I'm paying attention, so I make the extra effort to go and source Novavax, but my co-workers are busy busy busy. They have so many things pulling them in a lot of directions, they take the first vaccine that they come across. Then immunity wanes, they catch Covid from their kids in school who bring it home, and then for the rest of the winter months they're catching everything they come across with their possibly weakened immune systems.

Thank you.

title: Comment from COPD Foundation
comment:
On behalf of the COPD Foundation and our constituency of lung health patients, their caregivers, loved ones, and health care providers, we appreciate the opportunity to address the CDC’s Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP). The COPD Foundation’s mission is to help millions of people live longer and healthier lives by advancing research, advocacy, and awareness to stop chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), bronchiectasis, and nontuberculous mycobacterial (NTM) lung disease. The patient population that we serve is at an increased risk of significant complications from respiratory infections like RSV and pneumococcal disease.

We extend gratitude to the Committee for lowering the recommended age of pneumococcal disease vaccination to 50. Expanded access to pneumococcal vaccines is critical because pneumococcal pneumonia kills about 1 in 20 older adults who contract it. Expanding the age-based recommendation provided expanded access to potentially lifesaving prevention measures, including those in underserved communities that are most vulnerable and experience higher rates of pneumococcal disease.

It is anticipated that this expanded age-based recommendation will prevent pneumococcal disease in adults aged 50-64. This is a significant benefit to the lung health community, which is more likely to experience significant complications from bacterial illnesses. All adults over 50 can now speak with their healthcare providers or pharmacists about getting a pneumococcal vaccination. This vaccine can be administered at any time of year at the same time as other vaccinations. This vaccination is also free of cost to Medicare and Medicaid beneficiaries. The age-adjusted vaccine recommendation will reduce the chance that adults in this population will contract pneumonia, which is a “win” for our community.

We extend our support for the same measure to be applied to the RSV vaccine by lowering the recommended age of RSV vaccination to 50. An October 2024 Lancet study found that RSV vaccine effectiveness in adults over 60 both with and without immunocompromising conditions was over 73% against RSV-associated hospitalizations. Not only will increasing the age range in which adults can receive the RSV vaccine cover more vulnerable individuals but will also ensure that those in a younger cohort will receive protection while their immune systems are still able to produce a robust response. Prevention is an extremely important facet of healthcare, especially for patients who are predisposed to medical complications. Appropriate vaccinations are key to preventing exacerbations, hospitalizations, loss of income, and death in patients with chronic lung disease.

title: Comment from Gallay, Mark
comment:
Public Comment on the Renewal of Authorization for the Novavax COVID-19 Vaccine

Submitted by: Mark Gallay

Introduction:

I am writing in strong support of renewing the authorization for the Novavax COVID-19 vaccine. I submit this comment not only as a concerned citizen but also as the primary caregiver for my elderly mother. My caregiving experience has provided firsthand insight into the real-world implications of vaccine tolerability and accessibility for medically vulnerable populations.

Background:

This comment pertains to the proposed renewal of the Emergency Use Authorization (EUA) or full FDA approval for the Novavax COVID-19 vaccine, which offers a non-mRNA alternative to the Moderna and Pfizer-BioNTech vaccines. This protein-based platform, using recombinant spike protein and Matrix-M adjuvant, represents a proven vaccine model with decades of safe application in other immunizations, such as those for hepatitis B and pertussis.

Analysis:

Clinical trial data have shown that Novavax’s COVID-19 vaccine has an efficacy rate of approximately 90% in preventing symptomatic COVID-19. Notably, it has demonstrated a favorable safety and tolerability profile. Reports from clinical trials and real-world administration suggest that Novavax recipients experience lower rates of systemic side effects—such as fever, chills, and fatigue—compared to those receiving mRNA vaccines.

This reduced reactogenicity is crucial for patients like my mother. After her last mRNA booster, she experienced distressing side effects that included acute confusion and debilitating fatigue, which persisted for days and disrupted her care routine. For individuals with neurological decline and fragile systems, such side effects can lead to hospitalization or accelerate cognitive and physical deterioration. The availability of a gentler alternative like Novavax provides a viable pathway to maintain protection against COVID-19 without imposing avoidable harm.

The agency’s commitment to a diversified vaccine portfolio is not only scientifically sound but also ethically necessary. Novavax addresses gaps in public health strategy by improving vaccine accessibility for those who are medically ineligible or reluctant to receive mRNA-based formulations. Removing this option would diminish our ability to equitably protect diverse populations, including older adults, the immunocompromised, and those with complex care needs.

Recommendations:

I respectfully recommend the following:
1.Renew the Novavax COVID-19 vaccine authorization to ensure continued availability of a safe, effective, and tolerable alternative to mRNA vaccines.
2.Preserve vaccine diversity in the U.S. portfolio to support informed medical choice, especially for vulnerable individuals who cannot tolerate mRNA platforms.
3.Consider the role of Novavax in long-term booster strategy planning, particularly for populations at elevated risk of adverse reactions.

Conclusion:

In summary, Novavax’s COVID-19 vaccine provides a critical option for people who cannot safely or comfortably receive mRNA vaccines. My own experience as a caregiver for a medically fragile loved one underscores how vital it is to have access to this gentler, yet highly effective alternative. I urge the agency to renew authorization for Novavax and continue offering Americans vaccine choices that reflect the diversity of real-world medical needs.

Thank you for your consideration and for the opportunity to participate in the regulatory process.

Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

title: Comment from Noznesky, Elizabeth
comment:
To the CDC ACIP committee:
I strongly believe that routine vaccination with FDA-approved and authorized vaccines saves the lives of both children and adults and improves quality of life. I believe this because I have read scientific studies and I know intimately the benefits of vaccination and the cost of not being vaccinated against common infectious diseases.
In 2008, I had to remove a lesion on my cervix due to HPV infection. After years of follow-up, I was declared free of lesions and the virus that caused them. However, the surgery weakened my cervix and caused concern during my pregnancy.

Changing RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination for all people between age 50 and 74 - not just those who are in specific risk groups.
Strengthening the HPV vaccination recommendation to encompass all ages covered by the current approval without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45.
Updating COVID and influenza vaccines for all ages and making them available earlier in the year (by July or August at the latest)
Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.
Sincerely,
Liz Noznesky

Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,

Jordan McClure

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
April 11, 2025

To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life.

Public health is being deliberately dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political and deliberate disinformation purposes.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines AND Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,

J. Smythe-Rivers

Novavax's clinical trial results have been very impressive with regard to safety and efficacy, and I attribute the fact that I still haven't contracted covid to the number and frequency of Novavax boosters I've had (in addition to my other precautions).

It's also important to keep a non-mRNA vaccine available as an option for people who have adverse reactions to the mRNA ones, or who may be reluctant to take them for any number of other reasons.

Please grant Novavax BLA approval, and make it available for pediatric use.
I also ask that you consider extending the expiration date from three to six (or nine) months, now that it's been around long enough to know that it can last without spoilage for that length of time. This would help increase access, and prevent waste.
Prevention of waste is especially important to consider for disaster preparedness here and abroad, because when a facility experiences a power outage for an extended time, Novaxax can be stored in a regular refrigerator, and could be kept viable with generators for a longer period of time than the mRNA ones that need super cold storage. Many other countries look to the U.S.'s recommendations to set their policies because they don't have the scientific expertise or review infrastructure we have, and making these evidence-based changes could help protect public health around the world.

Thank you for consideration of my comments.

- Fully approve the Novavax COVID-19 vaccine through the Biologics License Application (BLA)
- Approve Novavax for pediatric use in children 6 months to 11 years
- Extend the shelf life of Novavax to 6 or 9 months
- Continue to ensure access and recommendation for the childhood vaccine schedule
- Continue to ensure access and recommendation for influenza vaccines

Access to Novavax is so important for all, but especially for immunocompromised, disabled, and allergic patients for which the mRNA vaccines are not an option. For me personally, I would need to take 4 or more days off of work with each mRNA dose, and I’ve experienced much lesser side effects with Novavax.

I urge you to continue to make decisions and recommendations based on sound, peer-reviewed scientific research, not opinion. Vaccines have saved countless lives. Thank for your time and commitment to ethical recommendations.
