title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Docket: CDC-2025-0017-0001

April 11, 2025

Public Comment for the April 15-16, 2025 meeting of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices

To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives. As an immunocompromised individual who has small children this issue is of critical concern for me. Not allowing my kids to be vaccinated would expose me to great risk and possibly death.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,

title: Comment from Meyers, Eliana
comment:
I work closely and daily with people with various diagnoses and immune systems. I love and spend time with people who would be severely debilitated if they got Covid (again). Some have complications form Long Covid and some have other existing conditions. I also just can’t afford to miss too much work from being sick!

This vaccine has allowed and would continue to allow people to exist in the world (stay alive at all, socialize, work, contribute to society, etc). Please, we beg and demand, keep it accessible and affordable. It offers so much to soo many.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Due to my health issues, I'm unable to have an mRNA vaccine. That means my only form of protection from COVID is getting the Novavax vaccine. Please give full approval to Novavax, and have it available to people of all ages in time for Fall 2025. Thank you.

title: Comment from Jacob, Marshall
comment: I am in complete favor of approving this version of Novavax. It would make boosters more accessible to a wider range of people. Many people with long covid have a difficult time handling side effects from the mRNA, while their side effects are not as severe with the Novavax. Please approve it.

title: Comment from Herrick, Stephanie
comment: I'm writing to ask that the committee support continued approval and availability of Novavax.  I have found that the side effects are much milder than those from Pfizer or Moderna covid vaccines, and like to be able to have a choice about what goes in my body.  Please extend Novavax's BLA approval and expand it's range for pediatric use.  Extending the expiration date from three to six or nine months would also help people have access to it and prevent waste.  Thank you.

title: Comment from Ackerson, Tammy
comment:
Given the research on the Novavax COVID-19 vaccine demonstrating that the vaccine is safe and effective, and also conveys more durable immunity than its mRNA counterparts, it should be approved for routine use.

Given that the Novavax COVID-19 vaccine is shown to have markedly fewer side effects than its mRNA counterparts, even in people where vaccine sensitivity is a serious concern, it should be made available as an option, to allow those seeking immunization a meaningful choice in which vaccine to take.

title: Comment from H., Mariana
comment:
CDC ACIP committee:

I join members of the scientific and public health communities to urge you to support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency. I ask that you continue to have ongoing open meetings of advisors who represent the majority science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Please do not allow politicians to get in the way of vaccine approval and recommendation. I read that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors--this is unacceptable.

Moreover, I support the overwhelming scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a wide variety of pathogens saves the lives. You are likely aware that public health is being dismantled from every angle and misinformation is being disseminated rampantly (included by elected officials). It's appalling that we could go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I demand that members of this committee publicly support the safe and effective vaccines that we currently have, and I invite anyone accessing these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

Additionally, I have some comments on other items for your agenda:
1. For the voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74.
2. We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s also important that these vaccines be made available earlier than in previous years to avoid late summer surges.
3. Flu vaccines must also be updated for fall 2025 and be made available for all ages.
4. Please strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45.
5. I ask that you support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future.

I appreciate your time and ongoing efforts.

title: Comment from Biko, Alivia
comment: Immunizations/vaccines are life-saving. Please support their user! Thank you.

title: Comment from Taylor, Megan
comment: Vaccines are not just rigorously tested for safety and efficacy, they are a crucial part of preventative healthcare. They have significantly reduced the death caused by a wide range of horrific diseases, as well as making the disease much less deadly if a person does catch it. Vaccines protect the most vulnerable people in our society - babies, the elderly, immune compromised people who rely on the protection of herd immunity to keep them safe. They allow our military to operate at peak efficiency - without the threat of sickness. A healthy nation is a strong nation.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Speaking as somebody with Long Covid, Novavax has been a godsend. I encourage all my friends & family to get whatever vaccine they choose: I have nothing against the other vaccines. But what you must understand is that for me and others like me, Novavax is the only option. Please don't remove our ability to get vaccinated. It's also the vaccine of choice for those who are hesitant about the mRNA vaccines: talking about the Novavax as a classic vaccine more widely might help boost vaccine adoption. Permitting Novavax would be such a huge win for so many dimensions of public health! Thank you so much for your time and consideration.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Vaccines, and other science-based public health efforts are NECESSARY to keep us all healthy!  Please keep that in mind.

title: Comment from Schmidt, Laura
comment: I am writing to ask for Novavax as a vaccine option and for Novavax BLA approval as well as expansion for pediatric use.  Initially, my family and I had the mRNA options for covid vaccines, experiencing the typical vaccine side effects of fatigue, headache and soreness, however, when Novavax was approved, we switched and experienced ZERO side effects. WE should have the right to choose what medicines and vaccines we choose to put in our bodies!!! I and my family choose Novavax!

title: Comment from Crum, Lotetta
comment: Please renew approval of the Novavax COVID vaccine. It is a safe, effective vaccine and a valid alternative for those who wish to avoid mRNA products. I have tried both types and vastly prefer Novavax for it's milder side effects in my experience.

title: Comment from Fuellemann, Lisa
comment: Please approve the 2025 version of Novavax. Patients who are contraindicated for mRNA vaccines deserve protection and alternative options to Moderna and Pfizer. Personally, I have had Covid vaccines from Johnson & Johnson, Moderna, Pfizer, and Novavax.   Of them, Novavax caused the least amount of unwanted side effects and I hope that it continues to be offered.

title: Comment from Richard, Holly
comment: Please approve Novavax’s BLA--my family has been able to access Novavax through our Costco and has had better reactions to this vaccine than the other ones. We rely on Novavax vaccines to lessen the severity of covid infections and keep us out of the hospital. We have been waiting for years for pediatric use and hope that this can be approved sooner than later. Our older child handles Novavax so much better than Pfizer and Moderna; it's hard to watch our younger child be sick after the Pfizer vaccination. We'd really like to have choices, especially when Novavax has been proven to be more effective at lessening the severity of covid.  Finally, we'd love for the expiration date to be moved from three to six or nine months, so our pharmacies can keep them on hand and not have to waste. Thank you.

title: Comment from Powell, Jennifer
comment: Please approve the 2025 version of Novavax for patients who are contraindicated for mRNA vaccines. Patients who cannot tolerate mRNAs deserve protection and another effective option.

comment: Please approve the 2025 Noxavax vaccine for use. From the research I’ve seen, it appears to be more effective long-term and also be better for those who have Long Covid than MRNA vaccines. This would be a major step in continuing to keep our community safe from Covid 19

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Please reauthorize the Novavax vaccine. For those of us who are immune compromised it is the best option and has been shown to be safe and effective.

title: Comment from Oross III, Ph.D, Stephen
comment: As a heart transplant patient, I am largely unresponsive to vaccines which can assist me in avoiding a potentially fatal infection from COVID. After trying all versions, only Novavax showed any success in providing protection for me. Given that I am often in high- risk settings due to my position as a university professor, I strongly encourage the approval of the updated Novavax vaccine. My life, and the lives of many others, may literally rely on this vaccine.

comment: I have serious lung disease. Were it not for vaccinations? I would’ve died last year had I contracted Covid or communal pneumonia. The anti-vaxers are endangering millions of people. There is no legitimate reason to end vaccinations. Those arguing against vaccinations are simply wrong and very very dangerous people.

title: Comment from S, Mara
comment: Please invest in and prioritize updating and distributing vaccines. I'm disabled and I rely on updated COVID boosters every 5 to 6 months, especially Novavax because it has fewer side effects. This is a very important piece of public health!

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: Novavax is my family's favorite booster shot. We got mRNA shots initially but switched to Novavax in 2023 and 2024. Please approve the 2025 version of Novavax. There is good data on Novavax's effectiveness, and it has very minimal side effects for me and my family.

title: Comment from Komarow , Elaine
comment: Please approve the 2025 Novavax COVID vaccine. Novavax is better tolerated by many people and the more options we have for people who choose to get vaccinated the better.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: We want Novavax as a COVID-19 vaccine option. It is the best, most effective vaccine against COVID-19 and American people deserve to make their own decisions about which vaccines to take. Not only does Novavax need to be BLA-approved by the FDA, but the expiration date needs to be expanded from three months to nine months and it needs to be expanded for pediatric use.

title: Comment from Mulligan, Sean
comment:
My name is Sean Mulligan and I have been vaccinated with Novavax since Fall 2023. In previous years I received Moderna and had extreme side effects and lost time at work because of extreme fatigue, chills, and arm pain.
According to Yale Medicine "The Novavax vaccine is the only non-mRNA updated COVID vaccine that has been available in the U.S. This vaccine is a protein adjuvant that had a 90% efficacy in its clinical trial, performing almost as well as the mRNA vaccines in their early trials. It is simpler to make than some of the other vaccines and can be stored in a refrigerator, making it easier to distribute."

Please approve the Fall 2025 formulation of the Novavax vaccine. It has been proven safe and effective and please give full authorization instead of emergency use. I am aware of RFK Jr's disapproval, however he is not a scientist and promoting vitamins instead of vaccines in the measles crisis is proof that he is not qualified to decide which vaccines are most effective in preventing severe outcomes from COVID-19.
Listen to scientists such as the former CDC Director Robert Redfield who supports Novavax. Also Michael Osterholm director of CIDRAP is quoted in Fortune Magazine "Novavax has the potential to offer longer, broader protection than current mRNA vaccines."

title: Comment from Davis, Lydia
comment: Please approve the Novavax vaccine! As a chronically ill person, I believe Novavax would be a better option for my health and the health of others like me. I would love to able to have this vaccine as another option to choose from, especially since it seems from initial research it has less side effects for chronically ill people and might have extended effectiveness. I want to stay as healthy as possible and take care of myself, and I believe this vaccine could help me do that. Thank you for your consideration!

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment: I urge you to continue to make Novavax available to people who want to get vaccinated against COVID-19. COVID is an ongoing threat to many people's health, especially those who are immunocompromised, chronically ill, living with Long COVID, disabled, and in a high risk population, such as seniors & the elderly. Novavax is a wonderful choice of vaccine because many people find it gentler to their bodies and experience fewer side effects as compared to mRNA vaccines. Unfortunately, not many people even know about Novavax and that's probably why more people choose an mRNA vaccine. If they knew it was a superior vaccine in terms of side effects, many more people would likely try it. Those who avoid COVID vaccines because of previous side effects or concerns about mRNA vaccines might choose Novavax if they were aware it exists. Many people are unable to tolerate mRNA vaccines due to very intense side effects and they deserve to have a choice about which vaccine they take especially since that option exists. Please continue approving Novavax for the upcoming fall vaccine season. Not having that choice will put many high risk people at risk of severe complications or death if they cannot stay up-to-date on their vaccines. Being unvaccinated will certainly put more stress on hospitals and healthcare systems. I'm an individual who lives with a chronic illness and I rely on Novavax for my vaccination because unlike mRNA vaccines, I've found I have no side effects other than a very mild sore arm when I take this vaccine. Thank you for taking my comment into consideration when you are making this very important decision.

comment: Thank you for allowing public comments. I am requesting that Novavax be kept as a COVID-19 Vaccination option. It is important to the public that we have a choice in our vaccinations. Please keep this vaccine as a viable option for the public! Thank you!

April 11, 2025

Public Comment for the April 15-16, 2025 meeting of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices

To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, especially protein based Novavax, and mRNA Pfizer and Moderna vaccines. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age missed the opportunity for vaccination. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also demand ongoing Project NextGen funding through ASPR/BARDA to accelerate development of safe and effective pan-coronavirus and influenza vaccines. Dozens of vaccine candidates have been in Phase-1 and Phase-2 trials and people deserve to receive the benefits of tax-payer funded research and FDA/CDC EUAs to help rapidly deliver them.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

title: Comment from Enerson, Hal
comment:
I am writing in support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

I must note that is regrettable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life.

It is outrageous that funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines.

No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured.

For the meeting’s voting items, I would like to recommend the following:

- I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

- We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall.

- Influenza vaccines must also be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

- I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45.

- Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future.

In closing, I need to point out that all of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons. Vaccine approval and recommendation MUST occur without any threat of political interference.

Thank you to all committee members for your hard work and ongoing support of vaccination.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

As an older American in particular ongoing access to Covid, Pneumonia, Influenza and RSV vaccines are essential to continue living healthily and not becoming a burden on family or hospitals!

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax.

All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year.

It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely, Katharine Constas
High Falls, NY 12440

If Novavax is not approved for the 2025-2026 cycle, millions of Americans with chronic medical conditions (including, but not limited to, long Covid) will be unable to be vaccinated against repeat infections--potentially costing them their health, and causing even more economic harm through increased illness and disability at population scale. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

title: Comment from Vaccinate Your Family
comment:
Health and Human Services (HHS) Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
Docket No. CDC–2025–0017

Vaccinate Your Family submits this comment to reaffirm the importance of the vaccines recommended by the ACIP in safeguarding our nation’s health and to call on the Administration to uphold the integrity of vaccine regulatory pathways and safety systems.

Vaccinate Your Family (formerly named Every Child By Two) was formed on the heels of the measles epidemic of 1989-90 which grew to over 50,000 cases. Our co-founders, former First Lady Rosalynn Carter and Betty Bumpers knew that the 150 lives lost to a vaccine-preventable disease were too many and so they dedicated their lives to vaccine advocacy. VYF has been a nonpartisan voice for 35 years, working with the White House and Congress to protect all of our communities. The United States finds itself at another critical moment – children are once again dying from measles, a vaccine-preventable disease.

From the very top of the government response, efforts must be focused on saving lives with the best tools we have – vaccines. Instead, our organization fears that the HHS Secretary’s slow response to the outbreak and dismantling of HHS now takes the shape of an ideological pursuit to undermine our nation’s immunization programs. This is evidenced through the defunding of public health, mass firings of experts, disruption of regulatory pathways, and the hiring of discredited researchers to manipulate safety data to prove discredited theories. All of this is resulting in the erosion of public confidence in our nation’s stellar vaccine safety systems, contributing to the deaths of children from diseases these vaccines can prevent.

Vaccinate Your Family is pleased this ACIP meeting is happening. Health officials including the HHS Secretary are calling for increased transparency; holding public meetings like this convening of the ACIP and the regularly scheduled VRBPAC meetings at the FDA is how we keep our nation’s vaccine approval, recommendation and ongoing safety review process transparent. Unfortunately, VRBPAC meetings have been cancelled without any information on scheduling for 2025. The federal process of reviewing vaccine safety and efficacy data and making recommendations is already less transparent than it was this time last year.

In support of transparency and keeping conflicts of interest out of our vaccine recommendations, it’s important to note that ACIP members are and have been subject to robust disclosure policies. Members make annual financial disclosures, are required to divest from vaccine manufacturer stock, and there is a prohibition on accepting industry payments. We have personally witnessed members recusing themselves from any vote in which they may be conflicted. Occasionally, ACIP members do disclose conflicts when they’ve worked on a study, held a patent, or are otherwise involved in the creation of or monitoring of a specific vaccine. The expertise of these individuals is essential, as we need the people who understand vaccines best to serve on the ACIP.

As of the writing of this comment, two children have died in Texas from measles – the first pediatric measles deaths in our nation in over two decades. Parents choosing not to vaccinate want to keep their families safe, as we all do. They believe they are making a safer choice by choosing not to vaccinate –but unfortunately this is not what the data show, and fear is costing lives. It is true that vaccines, like all medical interventions, come with the risk of rare side effects, but outsized fears are being allowed to grow rather than being right-sized by those in leadership. This is costing lives today.

At ACIP, experts sit down with the best available data to examine the risks of not vaccinating vs. the risks of vaccinating and ONLY recommend a vaccine when the benefits outweigh the risks. Again, we have witnessed this process for three decades and trust in the decisions rendered by this body. We’ve heard from the new administration that they support “informed consent” in vaccination – an informed choice to vaccinate is made when someone understands the benefit of a vaccine as well as the potential risks (and how small they truly are as compared to the risk associated with their child contracting measles, for example). ACIP meetings reviewing robust safety and efficacy data make informed consent possible.

When vaccines work (as well as the safety systems, regulatory meetings, and delivery systems that support them), nothing happens. People are protected and their families remain safe from deadly preventable diseases. Vaccines are a victim of their own success. The ongoing measles outbreak, as well as whooping cough and flu deaths, are stark reminders that our nation's public health is hard fought and fragile. We thank the ACIP members for their work to make sure vaccines remain safe, effective and recommended appropriately.

title: Comment from Miller, Erin
comment:
Attn: Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices,

I am writing to express my strong support for the approval of the Novavax COVID-19 vaccine (NVX-CoV2373). In light of ongoing COVID-19 challenges and recent public discourse, approving Novavax is a critical step in enhancing our vaccination strategy.​

COVID-19 Remains a Serious Threat:

Despite the lifting of emergency declarations, COVID-19 remains a leading cause of death and disability in the U.S., with over 1 million Americans lost and tens of millions more affected by complications. As new variants continue to emerge and re-infection becomes more common, the need for a diverse, effective, and accessible vaccine portfolio remains critical.

Addressing Vaccine Hesitancy:

Vaccine hesitancy poses a challenge to achieving widespread immunization. Offering Novavax, a vaccine developed using traditional protein subunit technology, provides an option for individuals who may be hesitant about newer vaccine platforms. This approach can help build trust and encourage vaccination among those who have concerns about mRNA vaccines.​

Effectiveness Against Emerging Variants:

Novavax has continued to adapt its formulation to address new variants, including XBB.1.5 and JN.1. Studies show meaningful immune responses when used as a primary series or as a booster, even in those previously vaccinated with other platforms. This cross-platform flexibility adds important resilience to our national immunization strategy.

Addressing Public Misinformation:

Recent statements by public figures, such as Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., have raised concerns about vaccine efficacy and safety. While open discussion is essential, it's important to rely on rigorous scientific evidence. Studies have consistently shown that vaccines, including Novavax, are safe and effective in preventing COVID-19. Spreading misinformation can lead to vaccine hesitancy and undermine public health efforts.​

The Growing Burden of Long COVID

An often-overlooked aspect of the pandemic is long COVID. Recent estimates indicate that over 4.4 million Americans are currently disabled due to long COVID, experiencing symptoms such as fatigue, cognitive dysfunction, and cardiovascular problems months after their initial infection. Among those with long COVID, over 65% report at least one significant disability, affecting their ability to work, care for others, or participate in daily life. Long COVID has been a key contributor to workforce disruption and economic strain, with an estimated $1 trillion in global productivity loss annually. Preventing even a portion of these cases through effective vaccination would yield massive public health and economic benefits. Additionally, when receiving an mRNA vaccine/booster, many individuals with long COVID experience intense crashes altering their already limited baseline function. Novavax, fortunately, is not known to elicit these negative effects on quality of life.

Conclusion

Approving the Novavax COVID-19 vaccine for broader use is essential not only to provide safe and effective immunization but also to reduce long-term disability caused by COVID-19. With its traditional technology, robust safety data, effectiveness against variants, and ability to appeal to hesitant individuals, Novavax offers an important solution at a time when public health cannot afford complacency.

Thank you for your continued commitment to safeguarding the health of all Americans.

title: Comment from SAFE Communities Coalition
comment:
April 11, 2025

Stephanie Thomas
Committee Management Specialist
Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices
National Center for Immunization and Respiratory Diseases
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
1600 Clifton Road NE, Mailstop H24-8
Atlanta, GA 30329

Attn: Docket No. CDC-2025-0017

Dear Ms. Thomas,

On behalf of SAFE Communities Coalition, a national organization dedicated to promoting public health and vaccines through education, advocacy, and community engagement, I am writing to provide our comments regarding the critical work of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP). We are proud to represent 11 nonpartisan Families for Vaccines advocacy chapters across the nation, working tirelessly to empower families with accurate information about vaccines and to advocate for policies that support immunization efforts.

Vaccines are an essential innovation that have significantly enhanced the quality of life for American families. They are a testament to the power of science and public health efforts in preventing outbreaks of diseases that were once common and often deadly. The protection that vaccines provide against diseases such as measles, mumps, rubella, and whooping cough is invaluable, ensuring that our communities remain safe and healthy.

As we continue to navigate the complex landscape of public health, it is imperative that we recognize the role of vaccines not only in individual health but also in community immunity. Widespread vaccination protects those who cannot be vaccinated, such as infants, the elderly, and individuals with certain health conditions. By supporting vaccination efforts, we are collectively creating a shield against infectious diseases that can devastate families and communities.

Moreover, vaccines symbolize an American innovation that has historically prioritized the health and well-being of our population. The ongoing research and development in the field of immunization should be supported and advanced, as it leads to the discovery of new vaccines and improved formulations that can further protect public health. It is crucial that we continue to foster an environment where scientific research thrives, ensuring that we remain at the forefront of disease prevention.

Access to vaccines is also a pivotal issue that requires our attention. We must ensure that vaccines are accessible and available to all segments of the population, irrespective of socioeconomic status. This includes providing resources and support to enable equitable access to immunization services, particularly in underserved communities. Furthermore, access to accurate and trustworthy information about vaccines is essential. Combatting misinformation is critical for empowering individuals to make informed choices about their health and the health of their families.

At SAFE Communities Coalition, we believe that our collective efforts in promoting vaccination and supporting strong public health initiatives will yield long-lasting benefits for all Americans. We commend the work of ACIP and urge continued collaboration to ensure the safety, efficacy, and accessibility of vaccines for every community.

Thank you for the opportunity to provide our comments. We look forward to ongoing engagement with ACIP to promote public health and strengthen our immunization efforts across the nation.

Sincerely,

Northe Saunders
Executive Director
SAFE Communities Coalition

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

title: Comment from Boucher, Solange
comment:
I am writing to support the committee’s commitment to scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. The health and wellness of our nation is more important than political partisanship. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA Vaccines and Related Biological Products Advisory Committee meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.This represents a dangerous departure from standard protocol that has the potential to put our nation’s most vulnerable citizens at risk. I support and believe in the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our overall quality of life. Public health is currently being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Funding for research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut under the guise of stopping censorship. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many United States communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. As a result, many childhood illnesses that were once endemic to our country have been seriously curtailed, saving the lives of countless children and supporting healthier communities across the nation. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary suffering, illness, disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write to their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

Furthermore, I wish to provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

Moreover, we need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well. I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that it is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

title: Comment from Nacewicz, Brendon
comment: I am writing as a psychiatrist (MD), clinic owner, and neuroscientist (PhD) with a background in neuroimmunology and the effects of viral vector delivery to the medial temporal lobe of nonhuman primates.  Our psychiatric intervention clinic serves vulnerable populations and about a third of our patients are on public insurance/Medicaid.  For the last two years, we have been fortunate enough to provide Novavax to our patients, in no small part due to the greater ease of handling and storage of a protein-based vaccine.  We would not be able to afford, nor do we have room for, a -70C freezer required for mRNA-based vaccines.  I also have followed the literature extensively since 2020, and clearly Novavax produces greater responses at barrier tissues and secretory IgA.  Even if the responses were equivalent, Novavax has little to no entrainment on recent/single strains that led to weakened immune responses in individuals given mRNA vaccines within 90 days of COVID infections.  Because of the greater generalization of antibody responses (e.g. neutralization of omicron from WT, neutralization of KP.2 from JN.1 antigent) than mRNA-based vaccines, it stands a greater chance of maintaining efficacy beyond the 5-6 months window of the latter.  Your committee knows this, just as it knows there are more side effects, including dangerous ones, from the mRNA vaccines.  I have seen patients with previous systemic reactions to mRNA vaccines (including a life-threatening pancreatits), who had virtually no problem with the protein-based Novavax.  Again, you can look at studies (https://www.journalofinfection.com/article/S0163-4453(24)00220-2/fulltext) or listen to my patients and staff, but either way Novavax is far more tolerable.  Without it, because of the logistics, the short duration of benefit, and the distrust of mRNA vaccines, any chance at herd immunity will likely be impossible.  Please approve this year's Novavax.

title: Comment from Jewell, Ari
comment:
I am commenting to support the committee’s commitment to scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. The health and wellness of our nation is more important than political partisanship. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA Vaccines and Related Biological Products Advisory Committee meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.This represents a dangerous departure from standard protocol that has the potential to put our nation’s most vulnerable citizens at risk. I support and believe in the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our overall quality of life. Public health is currently being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Funding for research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut under the guise of stopping censorship. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many communities in rural and under-served areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. As a result, many childhood illnesses that were once endemic to our country have been seriously curtailed, saving the lives of countless children and supporting healthier communities across the nation. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary suffering, illness, disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write to their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/under-insured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

Furthermore, I wish to provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I strongly suggest you consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

In addition, we need updated 2025-26 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well. I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that it is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many people who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

title: Comment from Smith, Margot
comment:
I grew up in the 1930s. My parents were fearful of epidemics. At school In first through 8th grades, I had classmates who suffered from scarlet fever, mumps, measles, German measles, chicken pox and whooping cough. I had rubella and had to stay in bed for several days in a darkened room; they thought light bad for sick children’s eyes. Several of these diseases required the family to put a quarantine sign on their door; their children missed a lot of school.

As an adult, I knew survivors—men who could not father children because they had mumps as a child, a woman with a flail arm from polio, people with chicken pox scars, those deafened because of measles, a man who spent 3 years in a tuberculosis sanitarium and a woman whose child was retarded because she had German measles during her pregnancy. I have friends who had polio then who now have post-polio syndrome, that is, muscle weakness, fatigue, and pain, for which there is no known cure. They experienced their illnesses before vaccines and antibiotics. My classmates at school were quarantined for these diseases. These are guidelines for how long diseases are contagious and possibly how long quarantines were needed:

•Poliomyelitis: 40 days
•Diphtheria: Onset of sore throat for up to 4 weeks.
•Measles: 4 days before rash until 4 days after rash appears
•Rubella (German Measles) 7 days before rash until 5 days after rash appears
•Chicken pox: 2 days before rash until all sores have crusts (6 - 7days)
•Scarlet fever: 21 days from the onset of the disease (in the absence of complications, 15 days)
•Mumps: 5 days before swelling until swelling gone (7 days)
•Whooping Cough: Onset of runny nose, 21 days after symptoms.
•Typhus: 12 days after the fall of temperature.

My mother was from eastern Europe (Chernowitz, Ukraine), born in 1893 and the youngest of eleven children. She knew only 8 of her sibs as her parents lost 3 sons to diphtheria in one week, years before she was born. At that time, 40% of children who caught diphtheria died.

These vaccines now protect my children and grandchildren from devastating diseases:
•1923diphtheria
•1924tetanus
•1940pertussis (whooping cough),
•1961poliomyelitis (polio),
•1963measles,
•1967mumps
•1969rubella (German measles)
•1994hepatitis B
•1995varicella (chickenpox)
We no longer fear epidemics and deaths from these childhood dangers because vaccination prevents them and so many people are vaccinated. Please make sure that people everywhere are vaccinated to keep us healthy.

title: Comment from PII, This is
comment:
Vaccines are a big part of why half of us aren't dead before we're 16. If you have to fight the powers that be to keep we the people safe, then do it. We need updated flu shots & covid-19 shots every year. Every 6 months in covid's case, but I've given up on that one. If there's a vaccine that works, it should be available, and health insurance companies should have to cover it, to protect the public, which is the whole point of the CDC, right? Right?

Because I'm just a member of the public who's childhood best friend became a pediatric ER physician, I will now defer to the person who made me aware of the comment period, and copy/paste what fits of her comment here. I've read through it, and everything I am capable of understanding about it, which is pretty much all of it, I agree with.

---

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

[...] Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

[...] No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

As a pathologist who specializes in cervical cancer screening, I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,

Kaitlin Sundling, MD, PhD

title: Comment from Kirby, Susannah
comment:

Public Comment for the April 15-16, 2025 meeting of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices
