I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Sincerely,

Susannah Kirby

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Docket: CDC-2025-0017-0001

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.
Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.
I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.
I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.
I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.
We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.
I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.
Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.
Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,
B

I couldn’t have taken care of myself to get food or healthcare. I was lucky to have a mom who could take care of me otherwise I understand how people can die. I’ve heard people say that vaccinations shouldn’t be mandatory and they should be left up to the parents. Without setting concrete expectations on a vaccine schedule and having requirements for vaccinations in schools, we will lose our immunity to these childhood diseases. People will needlessly die.

title: Comment from Smith, Cade
comment:
I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,

Cade Smith

Alondra Cruz

title: Comment from National Minority Quality Forum
comment:
April 11, 2025

Helen Keipp Talbot, MD, MPH
Chair, Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices
1600 Clifton Road, N.E., Mailstop H24-8
Atlanta, GA 30329-4027

RE: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, Docket ID: CDC- 2025-0017

Dear Dr. Talbot:

The National Minority Quality Forum (NMQF) is a 501(c)(3) not-for-profit research and advocacy organization based in Washington, DC. The mission of NMQF is to reduce patient risk by assuring optimal care for all. Our vision is an American health services research, delivery and financing system whose operating principle is to reduce patient risk for amenable morbidity and mortality while improving quality of life. NMQF’s forward-looking approach to population health operates at the nexus of the humanity-centric imperatives of the physical laws and the social contract.

The National Minority Quality Forum is submitting this public comment for consideration during the April 15-16, 2025, meeting of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP). Specifically, NMQF supports the development of recommendations that will help ensure that adults have timely and affordable access to technologies that prevent amenable morbidity and mortality associated with Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV).

Rationale for NMQF Public Comment

Respiratory Syncytial Virus is a common, highly contagious virus which circulates every autumn and winter in the United States. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevent (CDC), while most people recover in a week or two, RSV can be serious, especially for infants, older adults and adults with chronic medical conditions. RSV leads to about 2.1 million outpatient visits annually in the United States. For patients 65 or older, each year brings about 60,000–120,000 hospitalizations and 6,000–10,000 deaths. Long-term health consequences of RSV in adults include medical conditions like chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), asthma or congestive heart failure.

This public comment, therefore, reflects NMQF’s commitment to eliminating barriers to effective health services for the American general population, including, but not limited to, populations and geographic areas that are at increased risk for exposure to RSV, and to concomitant constrained access to essential clinical and public health services. NMQF is also responding to HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy, Jr.’s articulated ideal that immunization is a decision that should be made by individuals, families and their physicians. NMQF believes that ACIP’s deliberations, and the resulting recommendations from the CDC Director, can bring these values into alignment.
ACIP’s Role and Statutory Authority
As noted on the CDC website , ACIP provides advice and guidance to the Director of the CDC regarding use of vaccines and related agents for effective control of vaccine-preventable diseases in the U.S. civilian population of the United States. The HHS Secretary, and by delegation the CDC Director, are authorized under Section 311 and Section 317 of the Public Health Service Act, [42 U.S.C. §243 and 42 U.S.C. §247b], as amended, to assist states and their political subdivisions in the prevention and control of communicable diseases.
CDC sets the U.S. adult and childhood immunization schedules for civilian populations based on recommendations from ACIP. ACIP was established under Section 222 of the Public Health Service Act (42 U.S.C. §2l7a), as amended. ACIP is governed by the provisions of the Federal Advisory Committee Act, Public Law 92-463 (5 U.S.C. § 1001 et seq.), as amended; and by subsections 1928(c)(2)(B)(i) and 1928(e) of the Social Security Act (42 U.S.C. § 1396s(c)(2)(B)(i) and 1396s(e)) and subsection 2713(a)(2) of the Public Health Service Act (42 U.S.C. § 300gg-13(a)(2)).

In Closing

The most effective treatment for RSV is prevention in the form of vaccination. Effective, affordable vaccines are critical to prevent RSV infections and to eliminate avoidable costs for families, insurers and employers. The National Minority Quality Forum encourages ACIP to assign the highest valence to these public health benefits throughout its deliberations and votes during the April 15-16, 2025, meeting.

NMQF would be pleased to discuss this with you. If you have any questions, please contact Gretchen C. Wartman, NMQF Vice President for Policy and Program (gwartman@nmqf.org).

Sincerely,
Gary A. Puckrein, PhD
President and Chief Executive Officer
National Minority Quality Forum

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,
S. Allen

title: Comment from Farmer, Christina
comment: Full and unrestricted FDA approval for Novavax is needed now more than ever. title: Comment from Davies, Morgan
comment: I am commenting to express my support for the approval of the new Novavax COVID-19 vaccine. As someone with long covid, access to this vaccine has been incredibly important for me: while the traditional mRNA vaccines typically cause severe and sometimes dangerous side effects for those of us with this illness, my experience with Novavax has been entirely painless. I have received the Novavax vaccine twice and have experienced no side effects whatsoever. It is extremely important to me to have access to the COVID-19 vaccine given how dangerous it would be for me to get the virus again—currently, my long covid is so severe that I am unable to work and spend the vast majority of my day in bed—and I am very worried by the prospect of having to get a vaccine that could harm me (and others with this serious condition). I urge you to approve Novavax for patient use again this year.

title: Comment from Chang, Jennifer
comment:
I’m writing today in regard to the Novavax covid-19 vaccine. Evidence supports that Novavax should obtain full licensure, ending the emergency use authorization period. Please support BLA approval for Novavax. Please also support expanding the eligibility criteria of Novavax to include pediatric use. The vaccine expiration date should be reviewed, as other countries have successfully extended this to 9 months.

Having and maintaining multiple vaccine options for Americans to an endemic BSL-3 pathogen is important for public health and safety. Novavax is currently the only non-mRNA immunization option for covid-19. Unfortunately, mRNA vaccines have been widely misunderstood and mischaracterized over the past few years, to the extent that there has been State legislation proposing the banning of all mRNA vaccines. Many studies have shown that Novavax is a successful vaccine and that being vaccinated has far better outcomes with fewer adverse events than not. We are fortunate that we have an alternative option to mRNA vaccines to consider, BLA approval for Novavax is essential for maintaining broader access to quality medical treatments. Extending this option to pediatric use will help with more people feeing comfortable to vaccinate their kids.

It can be difficult to navigate vaccine safety and efficacy while they have become part of a highly charged political environment. I ask that committee members remember the value of the covid-19 vaccines and how much they have changed the prognosis of an acute infection. We may never have a 100% effective way to prevent people from getting infected with covid-19, but we also need to remember that this was not the standard set for these vaccines for good reason.

We must all commit to doing our part in reducing the negative effects of covid-19 infections, reducing severity and hospitalizations, and reducing the number of deaths. I ask that you please provide your support to broaden access to Novavax as a safe and effective vaccine against severe illness and outcomes from covid-19.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

As an oncology nurse, my patients are vulnerable to infections, and mass vaccination campaigns allow them to safely participate in their daily lives without fear of contracting many infectious diseases. Continued support of these critical immunization campaigns and research make it safer for all of us to participate in public life.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,

An oncology nurse in Philadelphia, PA

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
2025

Public Comment for the April 15-16, 2025 meeting of the

To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely, Donna

title: Comment from Ho, SoQuan
comment:
Vaccines are important, especially in the context of [Docket No. CDC–2025–0017], because they play a critical role in public health efforts to prevent the spread of infectious diseases—particularly among vulnerable populations like newborn children. This docket likely refers to a proposed CDC policy, recommendation, or program related to immunization practices.

Newborns are at higher risk of complications from vaccine-preventable diseases due to their underdeveloped immune systems. Ensuring timely vaccination, as outlined in CDC guidelines and supported by policies under such dockets, helps protect these children during their most fragile months of life. It also contributes to herd immunity, reducing the chances of outbreaks and protecting those who are too young or medically unable to receive vaccines.

In short, vaccines are a cornerstone of preventive healthcare and policies like those referenced in CDC dockets help ensure safe, effective, and widespread access to immunization, safeguarding public health from infancy onward.

title: Comment from Hedges, Valeri
comment:
As a concerned and informed citizen, I urge the CDC to take the following actions regarding Novavax’s COVID-19 vaccine:

1. Approve Novavax’s Biologics License Application (BLA) to expand access to a proven, protein-based COVID-19 vaccine that continues to demonstrate strong efficacy and durability.

2. Grant pediatric expansion to provide children and families with a critical non-mRNA option—especially important for those with contraindications or hesitations regarding newer technologies.

3. Extend the shelf life to 9 months, as available stability data supports this timeframe. Extending expiration dates will prevent unnecessary waste and support providers with better logistical planning.

4. Ensure updated Novavax vaccines are available by the end of summer alongside other updated options. This timing is crucial to allow people to make informed choices ahead of the fall respiratory virus season.

Data continues to show that Novavax offers strong and durable protection. In recent comparative studies, the updated Novavax vaccine demonstrated the highest effectiveness against symptomatic COVID-19 among available options, particularly against newer variants like JN.1. Additionally, its traditional protein-based platform may appeal to populations hesitant about mRNA vaccines, offering a critical choice in our public health strategy.

Expanding access to Novavax promotes equity, increases vaccine uptake, and supports long-term pandemic preparedness. Thank you for considering these actions in the interest of public health.

title: Comment from Matel, Michelle
comment: I support the approval of the 2025 version of Novavax. Much as we are helped by having several different versions of antibiotics so that if one isn't effective against a particular bacterial strain, another is available, so we should have several versions of the COVID vaccine. I understand that those individuals suffering from long COVID can have adverse reactions to the mRNA boosters where other boosters do not provoke the same reactions; since an individual can contract COVID more than once, it is important that those people have the protection available to avoid contracting new versions of the virus. An additional reason to approve it is that having competition in the vaccine market works to keep health costs down.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Docket: CDC-2025-0017-0001

April 11, 2025

Public Comment for the April 15-16, 2025 meeting of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices

To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am not a doctor or researcher, but I am an American who is following information on medical research and policy. I am personally very grateful for the excellent science behind all the vaccines that have protected me, my loved ones, friends, and much of the general public, from preventable diseases from childhood to this point, when I am approaching retirement. I want myself and others to continue to have access to these live-saving vaccines at the appropriate times of life, in line with recommendations based on high quality research.

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Life-saving vaccines are one thing that has made this country truly great. Money on medical research and prevention - including the administration of vaccines - prevents much larger costs in treatment of preventable disease, and lost economic opportunity due to illness, later on.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely, a concerned, science-loving citizen

title: Comment from Coil, Nina
comment:
To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,
Nina Coil, wife of an immune-compromised cancer patient

title: Comment from Benatar, Lisa
comment:
Two years before a Meningococcal B vaccine was approved in the United States
(October 2014), my husband and I lost our oldest child, Emily, to Meningitis B. We witnessed as our beautiful 19-year-old daughter in her freshman year of college lay screaming in pain in a neuro-ICU, then fell into a coma and underwent innumerable procedures that were excruciating to watch (and we don’t know how much of this she experienced). After 21 days, she died. Not a day goes by without me thinking of my daughter, and the painful memories of her final days will stay with me forever. Emily’s passing deeply impacted not just our immediate family but also her friends and their families, our extended family, and our community.
Death or disfigurement from meningococcal disease is not something you would wish on anyone, much less your own child or the child or grandchild of anyone you know. We are grateful that vaccines are now available to protect young people against contracting the disease, and the pentavalent (MenABCWY) meningococcal vaccine simplifies the process of providing this protection. My husband and I urge you to please prioritize your review of current meningococcal vaccination recommendations.
Very sincerely yours,
Lisa and Darrell Benatar
Palo Alto, CA

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
Docket: CDC-2025-0017-0001

April 11, 2025

Public Comment for the April 15-16, 2025 meeting of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices

To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to urge the committee to support approval of the Novavax COVID vaccine. Novavax offers a safe, highly effective alternative to the Pfizer and Moderna mRNA COVID vaccines. While the mRNA vaccines are also safe and highly effective, many people cannot take them due to allergic reactions and significant side effects. Novavax access is critical for those that have no other options, and provides additional freedom of choice to the American public in their vaccine options.

Personally, I have experienced high fever, extreme fatigue, body aches, and joint pain for 2-3 days following each dose of the mRNA vaccines, severe enough to require me to stay in bed. While I have been extremely grateful for the protection they offer, I cannot afford to take the better part of a whole week off from work every time I get a COVID vaccine. Novavax has been an amazing alternative for me, causing only a few hours of slight fatigue and injection site soreness that doesn't make me have to miss any work at all. I have no health conditions or allergies that would explain my reaction to the mRNA vaccines, and have found that this type of reaction is fairly common among my family and friends. When people are forced to choose between taking a vaccine and missing work due to extreme side effects or not getting vaccinated so they don't have to miss work, they will often choose to go unvaccinated, which is detrimental to both personal and public health.

I also urge the committee to generally support updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. Data show that COVID vaccine efficacy wanes after ~6 months for the mRNA vaccines, and is slightly more durable for Novavax. COVID vaccines should continue to be available every 6 months, or twice per year. The 2024 large summer COVID surge could have been avoided if COVID vaccines were available earlier than the fall, or if more groups were encouraged to be vaccinated every 6 months. Simplifying guidance to recommend vaccination twice per year would also decrease current public confusion about when different age groups should get boosted and in what circumstances. It is likewise imperative that Influenza vaccines continue to be updated for fall 2025, especially given the unusually bad flu season we have had this year.

Vaccine approval and recommendation cannot be disrupted by political interference. It is completely unacceptable that the recent FDA VRBPAC meeting was not open to the public and did not allow public comment or the participation of expert advisors. It is imperative that ACIP remains open to the public, continues to allow public comment, and continues to allow participation of a wide variety of experts.

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,
Kendrick Fowler

title: Comment from Lefebvre, Mark
comment:
o the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,

Mark Lefebvre

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,

(Your Name)

title: Comment from Toledo, Marianne
comment:
I am writing in support of 1) maintaining integrity of the vaccine approval and recommendation process, 2) Support for and explanation of current ACIP conflict of interest policies and 3) Not vaccinating against childhood illnesses is costing lives.
1) With renewed emphasis on transparency in our process – holding public meetings like VRBPAC and ACIP with independent experts is how we maintain the transparency in the process of vaccine review and approval. VRBPAC and ACIP meetings give the public a look at latest data on the safety and effectiveness
for all of the recommended vaccines as well as new vaccines in development. After working groups, the committee at large makes decisions after weighing multiple studies, and the quality of the data is always considered and interrogated. ACIP helps make sure our recommendations are based on the preponderance of evidence out there, not cherry-picked studies. This transparent process and the time and attention committee members spend reviewing high
quality data is how we can assuredly promote vaccines.
2) ACIP members are and have been subject to a rigorous disclosure policy designed to make sure they are NOT conflicted while serving on this committee. Disclosing their conflicts IS transparent and honest. In recordings of past meetings, you can hear and see when members have disclosed
potential conflicts. These meetings were public and disclosures are out in the open. The current policy for experts serving on ACIP requires: annual financial disclosures, divestment of vaccine manufacturer stock, prohibition on accepting industry payments, and that members recuse themselves from any vote in which they may be conflicted. VRBPAC and ACIP exist to uphold transparency and scientific rigor, and it is imperative that they continue to convene. I extend my thanks to current and past ACIP members for their time and expertise.
3) Families choosing not to vaccinate want to keep their families safe (as we all do). They feel that vaccinating would be a more dangerous choice – but this fear is unfounded and is costing lives. At these ACIP meetings, experts sit down with the best available data to literally weigh the risks of vaccinating vs not vaccinating and only recommend a vaccine if the benefits outweigh the risks. When vaccines work, nothing happens, people are protected and they remain safe from deadly diseases. Vaccines are a victim of their own success. We have forgotten what a world without vaccines looks like – a nation where children routinely die from illnesses like measles and polio. In addition, it may be convincing to consider the fiscal consequences of preventable disease outbreaks, like the current measles outbreak in Texas. These costs include medical care (clinic visits, medications, hospital care), public health response (contact tracing, large-scale vaccination clinics), lost wages and productivity.

For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely, Eli Romero Cruz

This - along with the fact that we know Covid vaccine immunity doesn't even last a full year - is why giving all ages access at least twice a year is essential.

The preventable deaths of two children this year as a result of the current measles outbreak - a disease that had been eradicated in the United States until anti-vaccine conspiracy theorists worked to sow doubt and fear about their safety and efficacy. title: Comment from Wallace, Robin
comment:
To the CDC ACIP committee:

I am writing to strongly support this committee’s scientific independence and transparency, including continued regular open meetings of advisors who represent science-based medical and public health professionals, as well as patient and public representatives.

Vaccine approval and recommendation must occur without any threat of political interference. It is unacceptable that a recent FDA VRBPAC meeting occurred behind closed doors, without the opportunity for public comment, and without the input of the usual membership of expert advisors.

I support the scientific consensus that routine vaccination with safe and effective FDA-approved and authorized vaccines against a broad spectrum of pathogens saves the lives of both children and adults and improves our quality of life. Public health is being dismantled from every angle, including mass firings of federal public health workers, and cuts to research and program funding. Under the guise of stopping censorship, research on vaccine hesitancy has been cut. Amid a growing measles outbreak where many US communities in rural and underserved areas have MMR vaccination rates that fall well below the threshold for herd immunity, funding to the highly successful and life-saving Vaccines for Children program has been cut. Notably, the Vaccines for Children program just celebrated its 30-year anniversary. We cannot go back to a time when infectious diseases ran rampant, causing unnecessary disease, disability, and death.

I urge members of this committee to speak out at every opportunity in support of our safe and effective vaccines, and I encourage everyone who reads these comments to write their elected officials to let them know that access to vaccines is an essential part of public health and disease prevention. No one will be healthier if safe and effective vaccines are not provided either through health insurance or through free programs for the uninsured/underinsured. All of us are harmed if public vaccine campaigns are dismantled for political reasons.

I will provide a few specific comments on additional items for this meeting’s agenda. For the meeting’s voting items, I urge you to consider simplifying the RSV vaccine recommendations to allow vaccination by age only, and not just for specific risk groups for people between age 50 and 74. Risk factors for severe disease within the 50 to 74 age group are common, and a weak recommendation creates uncertainty among both patients and vaccine providers. Many older adults receive vaccines at a pharmacy rather than at a medical clinic, and pharmacists may not feel comfortable assessing risk factors for patients who may not arrive with medical documentation or who may not have a regular primary care provider and thus may lack a specific high-risk diagnosis despite truly having a high-risk medical condition. Only 37.5% of high-risk adults aged 60-74 years had received an RSV vaccine, based on data as of March 29, 2025, whereas 47% of adults aged 75 and older had received an RSV vaccine. A simple age-based recommendation lowers barriers to vaccination for high-risk groups.

We need updated 2025-2026 COVID vaccines of all FDA-approved and authorized types, including Pfizer and Moderna mRNA vaccines as well as Novavax. All ages should have access to these vaccines at least twice a year. It’s essential that these vaccines should be made available earlier than in previous years, by July or August at the latest and prior to the start of K-12 schools in the fall. Influenza vaccines must be updated for fall 2025 and must be made available for all ages as well.

I urge you to strengthen the recommendation for HPV vaccination to encompass all ages that are covered by the current approval (through age 45), without the stipulation for “shared decision-making” for ages 27-45. People my age often missed the opportunity for vaccination due to the timing of release of the initial vaccines, and I am hearing that is often difficult for people in their late 20s through early 40s to get HPV vaccines covered by health insurance. HPV exposure is nearly universal, and there are still far too many children and adults who are not adequately protected against HPV-driven cancers, which can occur in the cervix, throat, anus, and penis.

Please also support development of safe and effective vaccines against CMV and appropriate vaccination recommendations, should such a vaccine be FDA approved within the near future. CMV is a common infection that can cause pregnancy loss, birth defects, and severe infections in immunocompromised people. CMV causes lifelong infection and can reactivate later in life when the immune system is weakened, such as during cancer treatment or bone marrow transplantation.

Thank you to this committee for your ongoing work in support of vaccination.

Sincerely,
Robin Wallace

title: Comment from Anonymous
comment:
**Public Comment for the April 15–16, 2025 Meeting of the CDC Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP)**

To the ACIP Committee,

I'm writing as an engaged member of the public who deeply values the role of vaccines in protecting our communities, especially the most vulnerable among us. I don’t have any professional affiliations or conflicts of interest—just a strong belief in science-based public health and the right of all people to access safe and effective vaccines.

One vaccine I want to especially highlight today is Novavax. For people who are immunocompromised or who have medical conditions that make mRNA vaccines unsuitable, Novavax represents a critical lifeline. It’s a protein-based vaccine—different from the mRNA options—and having it available helps ensure that those who can't take other vaccines still have protection against COVID-19.

This is not just a matter of personal health—it’s about public health and equity. When we only support one kind of vaccine, we leave behind entire groups of people. By making sure Novavax remains accessible and recommended alongside mRNA options, we help bridge gaps in care and provide real options for those who need them most.

It's especially important that Novavax gets approval for pediatric use. Children who can’t take mRNA vaccines or whose parents prefer a non-mRNA option deserve protection too. As schools reopen and COVID continues to circulate, having multiple vaccine choices, including Novavax, ensures more families can protect their kids and their communities.

Equity matters. Everyone—regardless of age, health status, or personal medical needs—should have access to the vaccine that’s safest and most appropriate for them. The more diverse our vaccine options, the more people we can reach. Protein-based vaccines like Novavax aren’t just a backup—they’re a necessary part of a fair and inclusive public health system.

I also want to urge the committee to push for timely rollout of all COVID vaccines for the 2025–2026 season. These vaccines should be available no later than July or August, so families can be protected before school starts. Waiting too long to distribute vaccines makes it harder for people to stay ahead of the virus.

Lastly, I support stronger vaccine recommendations across the board—whether for COVID, RSV, HPV, or future vaccines like CMV. Clear, age-based guidance helps remove barriers, especially for people without regular access to healthcare or insurance. Everyone benefits when vaccines are easy to get and when recommendations reflect real-world challenges.

Thank you for your work, and please continue to prioritize access, choice, and equity in every vaccine decision.

Sincerely,
Concerned citizen
